column2: Russian State-Sponsored Actors Linked to GRU Disrupted Ukrainian State Registries in Cyber-Attack on 19 December 2024
column3:
On 19 December 2024, a cyber attack attributed to Russian state-sponsored actors linked to the GRU temporarily disrupted Ukraine's state registries, managed by the Ministry of Justice. The attack, described as the largest of its kind on the country's registries, suspended online services for administrative matters like marriages, car registrations, and changes of residence.
Deputy Prime Minister and Justice Minister Olha Stefanishyna stated the attack aimed to "instill panic among Ukrainian citizens and those abroad" and noted it had been prepared for several months. While no data leaks or thefts have been confirmed, all registries remain offline as a precautionary measure. Restoration efforts are underway, with basic services expected to resume within two weeks. Ukrainian authorities, including the SSU, are conducting a criminal investigation, with suspicions centered on a GRU-affiliated hacker group.

column2: Unknown threat actors stole Microsoft Azure account credentials from automotive, chemical, and industrial manufacturing companies in Europe beginning in June 2024
column3:
Unit 42 researchers uncovered a phishing campaign targeting European companies, particularly in Germany and the UK, within automotive, chemical, and industrial compound manufacturing industries from June 2024 to at least September 2024. Palo Alto Networks reported the campaign and its findings on December 18, 2024.
The campaign aimed to harvest account credentials to take over victims' Microsoft Azure cloud infrastructure. Attackers used phishing emails containing malicious PDFs or HTML links created via the HubSpot Free Form Builder service. Victims clicking these links were redirected to credential-harvesting pages disguised as legitimate Microsoft login portals.
This campaign targeted approximately 20,000 users. Once credentials were harvested, attackers exploited them to access Microsoft Azure accounts, conduct lateral movements, and establish persistent control, even during active attempts to block their access. The attackers also used VPN proxies to mask their origins and mimic logins from trusted locations.

column2: Unspecified US intelligence agencies stole trade secrets from a a major Chinese high-tech enterprise since at least May 2023
column3:
The Chinese National Internet Emergency Response Center (CNCERT) reported two cyberattacks on December 18, 2024, in which the United States stole trade secrets.
In the second incident, suspected U.S. intelligence agencies have targeted a major high-tech enterprise in China's smart energy and digital information sector since May 2023. The attackers exploited vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange to hack into and control the company's mail servers, implanting backdoors to steal email data continuously. They also used the compromised mail servers as a springboard to attack and control over 30 devices belonging to the company and its subsidiaries. As a result, the attackers stole a significant amount of the company’s trade secret information. The incident involved using multiple overseas platforms to mask the attack's origin.

column2: Unspecified US intelligence agency stole trade secrets from an unnamed Chinese advanced materials design and research since at least August 2024
column3:
The Chinese National Internet Emergency Response Center (CNCERT) reported two cyberattacks on December 18, 2024, involving the theft of trade secrets by the United States.
In the first cyber incident, a Chinese advanced materials design and research unit has allegedly been targeted by U.S. intelligence agencies since August 2024. The attackers exploited a vulnerability in an electronic document security management system, compromising the company's software upgrade management server. Through this breach, they delivered control Trojans to over 270 company hosts via the software upgrade service, stealing significant amounts of trade secret information and intellectual property.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors breached the Attorney General's Office of Nuevo León in Mexico in March 2024
column3:
In March 2024, a hacker breached the computer system of the Attorney General's Office of Nuevo León, accessing 960 folders and stealing approximately 14.7 GB worth of information containing sensitive data, including photos of corpses and identifications. The attack was detected in March 2024, and an internal investigation was initiated. The Nuevo León Attorney General's Office kept the cyber incident hidden for nine months until the breach was revealed through social media. 
The incident was publicly disclosed on 17 December 2024 by the Attorney General's Office of Nuevo León. They claim to have gathered key evidence, including videos and documents, leading to the identification of possible perpetrators and the issuing of arrest warrants.

column2: Unknown threat actors disrupted Avril supermarket chain in Canada beginning 11 December 2024
column3: On the night of 11 December 2024, a cyberattack was directed towards the April supermarket chain, resulting in moderate impacts on the operations of the Granby-based banner. The attack caused long queues in the stores during the weekend of 14-15 December 2024, with a limited number of cases in service, and employees had to enter product codes manually. The company, with 13 addresses in Quebec, was conducting an in-depth analysis with cybersecurity experts to determine what has been affected in their systems. The attack has slowed down some activities, with some cases closed in all branches, but supermarkets remain open and operational. The incident was disclosed by Serge Gauthier, Executive Vice-President, in an email sent to Le Soleil on 17 December 2024. At the time of reporting on December 17, 2024, there is no indication that personal information from employees and clients was disclosed during the cyberattack.

column2: Ransomware group Hunters International stole data from French digital services company Ecritel on 8 December 2024
column3:
On December 8, 2024, French digital services company Ecritel faced a cyberattack claimed by ransomware group Hunters International on December 17, 2024. The group alleged the theft of 270 GB of sensitive data, including contractual documents, network architecture diagrams, IT administration tool interfaces, and Ecritel’s information system security plan (ISSP). Despite these claims, Ecritel reported that the attack was swiftly detected and thwarted without disrupting business continuity or customer platforms.
Ecritel's investigation, supported by a PASSI-certified third-party audit, concluded that only an internal server containing employee working documents was compromised and partially exfiltrated.

column2: Russian threat actor NoName057(16) launched DDoS campaign against German companies on 17 December 2024
column3:
On 17 December 2024, the Russian DDoS gang NoName057(16) launched a DDoS campaign against several German companies, including weapons manufacturer Walther, energy company Vattenfall Group, a wood-based materials manufacturer, automotive suppliers, and a construction chemicals company. The attackers temporarily disrupted their websites' accessibility, using the attacks as a political message rather than extorting ransom money.
The day before, the flood of parcels had targeted two German federal authorities, among others: The Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (BfDI) and the Federal Network Agency were allegedly attacked by a Russian support group.

column2: Unknown threat actors breached ConnectOnCall in the United States beginning on 16 February
column3: ConnectOnCall, a telehealth platform, was breached between 16 February 2024 and 12 May 2024, exposing personal and medical information of over 900,000 individuals. The breach, discovered on 12 May 2024, included names, phone numbers, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, medical record numbers, and health-related information. ConnectOnCall hired cybersecurity experts, notified federal law enforcement, and took its product offline to restore it in a secure environment. The company notified impacted individuals and offered them identity and credit monitoring services through Kroll, with notice letters mailed on 11 December 2024.

column2: Unknown threat actors hacked and stole data from the Facebook account of the municipality of Arauco in Chile by using a massive social media attack on 13 December 2024
column3: On 13 December 2024, hackers from China hacked the Facebook account of the municipality of Arauco, forcing officials to file a complaint with the Investigations Police of Chile (PDI). The attackers changed the fan page's name to 'Pet World', uploaded pet content in English, and stole data. The PDI informed the municipality of a massive attack from China targeting the social media platforms of public entities with many followers. The hackers' goal was likely to sell the accounts online. The municipality recovered some posts and created a backup account, 'Municipality of Arauco 2.0', to continue sharing content with their neighbours.

column2: South Asian threat actor TA397 (also known as Bitter) gained access to a Turkish defence sector organisation using WmRAT and MiyaRAT beginning since at least November 18, 2024
column3:
South Asian threat actor TA397 (also known as Bitter) gained access to a Turkish defence sector organisation using WmRAT and MiyaRAT beginning on November 18, 2024, U.S. IT-security company Proofpoint reported on December 17, 2024.
The attack began with a spear-phishing email containing a RAR archive disguised as a public infrastructure initiative by the World Bank in Madagascar. TA397 manually deployed two remote access trojans (RATs), WmRAT and MiyaRAT, to enable intelligence gathering and data exfiltration.
Proofpoint attributed this campaign to intelligence collection efforts likely supporting a South Asian government.

column2: Ransomware group Hunters International leaked customer data of Telecom Namibia on 10 December 2024
column3:
Hunters International ransomware gang breached the confidentiality of Telecom Namibia, Namibia's state-owned telecom provider, by conducting a ransomware attack at the end of November 2024. It resulted in the leak of over 400,000 files, including personal and financial data of high-ranking government officials and clients, after Telecom Namibia refused to negotiate a ransom, with the data being made public on the dark web. Some of it was circulating on social media, as reported by local media and confirmed by Telecom Namibia's chief executive, Stanley Shanapinda, on December 16, 2024.
Hunters International claimed the ransomware attack on December 10, 2024.

column2: Chinese state-sponsored Winnti Group targeted cybercriminals in China with Glutton backdoor beginning since at least April 29, 2024
column3: Beginning since at least April 29, 2024, Chinese threat actor Winnti Group was found to be using a PHP-based backdoor called 'Glutton' to target the cybercriminal underground, primarily in China. The backdoor, which shares similarities with the Winnti backdoor, was discovered by researchers at QiAnXin XLab, who reported the incident on December 12, 2024. Glutton was found to be embedded in an archive downloaded from the Timibbs cybercrime online market and linked to a fraudulent click-farming platform. The backdoor can extract sensitive system data, deploy an Executable and Linkable Format backdoor, and inject malicious code into PHP frameworks like Baota, ThinkPHP, Yii and Laravel. The attackers used poisoned tools to turn cybercrime operators into unwitting pawns, with the malware operating stealthily within PHP or PHP-FPM FastCGI processes. The victims of Glutton span various sectors, particularly in IT services and business operations, and include systems used by cyber criminals, with the majority of them located in China.

column2: The Serbian Security Intelligence Agency (BIA) accessed the mobile phone of an unnamed activist of the NGO Krokodil and stole information beginning on October 1, 2024
column3:
Amnesty International investigated a case involving an activist from the NGO Krokodil who suspected tampering with their phone following an October 2024 interview with Serbian BIA officials. Krokodil, an organization advocating for reconciliation in the Western Balkans and openly supporting Ukrainian and Russian dissidents, has faced frequent threats and vandalism, including a September 2024 incident where Russian-speaking intruders removed a Ukrainian flag from their office.
On October 1, 2024, after requesting a meeting with BIA regarding the incident, the activist was interviewed at the Ministry of Interior in Belgrade. Before the questioning, BIA officials asked the activist to restart their phone, which he unlocked using a pattern-based PIN. Although the officials could not visibly observe the unlock pattern, room cameras likely captured it. During the interview, which lasted from 10:17 to 11:40, the activist’s phone was left unattended in their jacket outside the room.
Upon leaving the interview, the activist noticed suspicious activity on their Samsung Galaxy S24+, including a notification that their contacts had been exported at 10:38. Confronting BIA officials yielded denials, prompting the activist to seek Amnesty International’s Security Lab assistance.
Forensic analysis confirmed that two spyware applications, NoviSpyAdmin and NoviSpyAccess, were installed on the phone during the interview. Logs revealed that at 10:20, the phone was powered on shortly after the interview began and connected to a ThinkPad X1 Yoga via USB. Authorities used the Android ADB protocol to gain control, disable security features, and manually browse the device, including its photo gallery, while exporting contacts.
NoviSpyAdmin, a device admin app, collected sensitive data such as call logs, contacts, SMS messages, and audio recordings. NoviSpyAccess exploited Android accessibility features to capture screenshots, track location, exfiltrate files, and activate the phone’s camera. By 10:27, NoviSpyAccess was configured as the active accessibility service, enabling comprehensive surveillance.
The spyware remained active on the device at the time of Amnesty International’s analysis, uploading screenshots of sensitive information, including emails, Signal and WhatsApp messages, and social media activity, to a spyware server. Data logs confirmed that NoviSpy communicated with IP addresses linked to Telekom Srbija, a government-controlled ISP.
Amnesty International reported on many cyber incidents against Serbian civil society. Amnesty International attributed this cyber incident to the Serbian Security Intelligence Agency
 (BIA). Amnesty International also wrote that Google was able to identify further incidents, i.e. incidents outside of the ones mentioned in the Amnesty International report. Amnesty International also assumes additional compromises based on the sample numbers of the malware.

column2: The Serbian Security Intelligence Agency (BIA) accessed the mobile phone of Serbian youth activist Nikola Ristic beginning on November 3, 2024
column3:
In November 2024, Amnesty International conducted a forensic analysis of the phone belonging to Nikola Ristić, a youth activist. The analysis confirmed that the phone was infected with NoviSpy spyware after being unlocked using Cellebrite UFED while in Serbian authorities' custody.
Nikola was a key organizer of a protest in Belgrade following the tragic collapse of a train station roof in Novi Sad, which killed 14 people and injured dozens on November 1, 2024. On November 3, Nikola and his partner arrived at Belgrade’s Trg Republike carrying red paint for the protest. Shortly after, they were stopped by four men who identified themselves as BIA officers. Despite Nikola’s initial resistance due to the absence of a warrant, he was taken to a police station after a brief altercation, during which an officer threatened him and acted aggressively.
Nikola’s belongings, including his phone, were confiscated at the station. During questioning, officers returned his phone and instructed him to call his partner to request the removal of a video of his arrest circulating on social media. After the call, his phone was taken out of the room. Nikola was interrogated; he perceived the questioning as a tactic to waste time, and no court order was presented to justify his detention. He was released after over two hours, around 1:30 PM.
Suspicious of tampering, Nikola contacted Amnesty International’s Security Lab. Forensic analysis of his Huawei Honor 20 Pro revealed traces of NoviSpy spyware installed during his detention, with evidence showing physical access to his device via the Android ADB protocol.  One week after the infection, the spyware was still active, and logs indicated that operators had reconfigured it to upload data every three minutes, showing active device management. The analysis also confirmed that Cellebrite UFED was used to unlock the phone before the spyware installation.
Amnesty International reported on many cyber incidents against Serbian civil society. Amnesty International attributed this cyber incident to the Serbian Security Intelligence Agency (BIA). Amnesty International also wrote that Google was able to identify further incidents, i.e. incidents outside of the ones mentioned in the Amnesty International report. Amnesty International also assumes additional compromises based on the sample numbers of the malware.

column2: Serbian Security Intelligence Agency (BIA) used Cellebrite tool and NoviSpy spyware to extract data from journalist Slavisa Milanov beginning on February 21, 2024
column3:
Amnesty International conducted a forensic analysis of the Android phone belonging to Serbian journalist Slaviša Milanov, who suspected tampering by Serbian authorities. Milanov, an investigative journalist from Dimitrovgrad working with the FAR news portal, focuses on sensitive topics such as local government spending and corruption.
On February 21, 2024, Milanov and a colleague were stopped by traffic police en route to a business meeting in Pirot. He was taken to the police station for additional tests for alcohol and drugs, which were conducted after a 20-minute delay and returned negative results. During his time at the station, Milanov was asked to leave his belongings, including his phone, and was later questioned by two men in civilian clothes about his journalistic work and the financing of FAR. Following the questioning, his devices were returned, but Milanov noticed unusual behavior on his phone, such as mobile data and Wi-Fi being turned off and excessive battery use by certain apps.
Suspecting unauthorized access, Milanov installed a security app, which revealed that some apps were accessed and new ones installed while he was in custody. Amnesty International’s Security Lab confirmed that NoviSpy spyware apps were covertly installed on his Xiaomi Redmi Note 10S during his detention. The analysis also found evidence that Cellebrite’s UFED forensic tool was used to unlock the phone, enabling the spyware installation.

column2: Unknown Hackers Breached LKQ Corporation’s Canadian Business Unit on 13 November 2024
column3:
On 13 November 2024, unknown threat actors breached the information technology systems of LKQ Corporation’s Canadian business unit, causing data theft and operational disruptions that lasted for several weeks. LKQ, a major U.S.-based provider of auto parts with over 1,600 locations and 45,000 employees worldwide, immediately launched an investigation, partnered with forensic experts, and notified law enforcement authorities.
While the affected business unit has since returned to near full capacity, LKQ disclosed the incident in a Form 8-K filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) on 14 December 2024. The company stated that the incident is not expected to have a material impact on its financial condition for the fiscal year and confirmed its plans to seek reimbursement through its cybersecurity insurance.

column2: Unidentified Cyber Criminals Breached Rhode Island’s RIBridges System in the US, Exposing Personal Data of Benefit Applicants and Recipients Starting on 5 December 2024
column3:
On 5 December 2024, unknown perpetrators targeted Rhode Island's public benefits system, RIBridges, managed by Deloitte, in a ransomware attack that compromised personal and financial data. The breach, confirmed on 10 December, exposed sensitive information, including names, addresses, birth dates, Social Security numbers, and bank details of individuals who applied for or received benefits such as Medicaid, SNAP, and TANF.
The state proactively shut down RIBridges on 13 December after discovering malicious code, halting the system's operations to contain the threat. Although no identity theft has been reported, Governor Dan McKee urged vigilance and offered free credit monitoring to affected households. A dedicated call center and website updates have been established to assist impacted residents

column3: On 27 November 2024, Argentina's National Atomic Energy Commission (CNEA) was targeted in a ransomware attack attributed to the Money Message strain, first identified in 2023. column2: Hacker 'hades_hgs' Breached HGS Database in Turkey, Stealing Personal and Financial Information of Over 5 Million Users, and Demanding Ransom on 14 December 2024
column3:
On 14 December 2024, Turkey's highway and bridge transit system, HGS, suffered a major cyber attack by the hacker group 'hades_hgs'. The attackers infiltrated the HGS database, stealing personal and financial data of over 5 million users, which was subsequently shared on the Dark Web. The group demanded a ransom of $25,000 and threatened to release additional sensitive information, including over a million credit card records, if their demands were not met. The attack is reportedly linked to a dispute involving a lawyer named 'Juadis.'
On December 16, 2024, various Turkish media reported on the detainment of two suspects linked to the beach of the Turkish Highway and Bridge Transit System (HGS). Involved law enforcement authorities, namely the Denizli and Mersin Provincial Gendarmerie Commands coordinated by the Department of Combating Cybercrimes and in cooperation with the National Intelligence Organization (MIT) and the Information and Communication Technologies Authority (BTK) and the Computer Emergency Response Team of Turkey (TR-CERT), detained Veli B. (also known as Hades) and Burak K. (also known as Klew) on suspicion of unauthorized access to information systems and sharing messages insulting national and religious values. During the investigation, Veli B. was arrested, while Burak K. was released under judicial control. It was noted that Burak K. admitted on social media that the cyber attack was carried out as a joke.

column2: RA World Ransomware Gang Stole 250GB of Internal and Customer Data From New Zealand Telecommunications Provider Compass Communications During a Ransomware Attack in December 2024
column3:
In December 2024, unknown threat actors, later identified as the RA World Ransomware gang, launched a ransomware attack against Compass Communications, a New Zealand-based telecommunications provider. The attack compromised both the confidentiality and integrity of the company's systems, with the attackers claiming to have exfiltrated 250 gigabytes of internal and customer data, including financial records, customer details, human resources files, and information on ongoing projects. As evidence of the breach, a 26.9-megabyte archive of sample data was posted on the gang's darknet leak site.
Compass Communications confirmed the breach to Cyber Daily, revealing that their security monitoring had detected unauthorised access to their systems the previous week. The company promptly engaged external cybersecurity experts and notified relevant government authorities. An initial investigation suggested that some customer information may have been accessed. The RA World Ransomware gang set a ransom deadline of 1 January 2025.

column2: Iranian state-sponsored hacking group CyberAv3ngers compromised critical infrastructure in Israel and the United States using IOCONTROL malware beginning in mid-October 2023
column3:
In late 2023 and mid-2024, Iranian threat actors, specifically the CyberAv3ngers group, used a new malware named IOCONTROL to compromise Internet of Things (IoT) devices and OT/SCADA systems in Israel and the United States, Claroty's Team82 reported on December 10, 2024. The targeted devices included routers, programmable logic controllers (PLCs), human-machine interfaces (HMIs), IP cameras, firewalls, and fuel management systems from various manufacturers, including D-Link, Hikvision, Baicells, Red Lion, Orpak, Phoenix Contact, Teltonika, and Unitronics. 
On October 14, 2024, the threat actors claimed to have compromised 200 gas stations in Israel and the U.S. on Telegram, which aligns with Claroty's findings. Claroty's Team82 confirmed that the threat actors compromised several hundred Israeli-made Orpak systems and U.S.-made Gasyboy fuel management systems. This happened in one go from mid-October 2023 to January 2024 until they resumed their attack from July to August 2024.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Disrupted Online Ordering Operations Of Krispy Kreme In The United States By Breaching Its Information Technology Systems On 29 November 2024

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Stole Credentials of Employees of 30 Companies Across 12 Industries Worldwide Beginning in Early July 2024
column3:
Beginning in at least July 2024, unknown threat actors launched a sophisticated global phishing campaign targeting employees of 30 companies across 12 industries, distributing over 200 malicious links designed to steal user login credentials, with stolen credentials sent to attackers in real time via C2 servers or Telegram bots. Cybersecurity researchers at Group-IB reported on 11 December 2024. 
The industries targeted in this phishing operation included Energy, Fashion, Finance, Aerospace, Manufacturing, Telecommunications, Government, IT, Healthcare, Technology, and Entertainment. 
This campaign was designed to bypass Secure Email Gateways (SEGs) and evade detection. In particular, the attackers used three main techniques to bypass email security: Trusted Domain Abuse, Dynamic Company Branding, Document Platform Impersonation. In the trusted domain abuse tactic, attackers embedded malicious URLs within legitimate services like Adobe.com and Google AMP, making it harder for security tools to detect. In document platform impersonation, fake DocuSign and Adobe notifications were used to lure users into clicking links for what seem to be important documents. Meanwhile, with dynamic company branding, phishing pages cleverly displayed the victims' company logo and branding by pulling them directly from legitimate websites. Users who clicked the malicious links were directed to convincing login pages pre-filled with their email addresses.

column2: Russian-Based Threat Actor Storm-1837 and Russian State-Sponsored Hacking Group Secret Blizzard (Also Known as Turla) Compromised Military-Related Devices in Ukraine in January 2024
column3: Since December 2023, Microsoft has been monitoring the activities of a Russian threat actor, Storm-1837 (overlapping with threat actors known as Flying Yeti and UAC-0149), targeting devices belonging to Ukrainian military drone operators. In January 2024, a Ukrainian military device was compromised using a Storm-1837 backdoor. The backdoor leveraged the Telegram API to execute a PowerShell cmdlet. This enabled connections to an account on the file-sharing platform Mega, likely for downloading files or commands. A PowerShell dropper observed on the device deployed two encoded files, delivering the Tavdig backdoor and a Symantec binary, enabling further reconnaissance. Secret Blizzard, another actor, used Tavdig to install the KazuarV2 backdoor for persistence and subsequent operations. Microsoft was still investigating whether Secret Blizzard leveraged access to Storm-1837's backdoor or purchased access, which would indicate cooperation. Microsoft assumed that Secret Blizzard likely wanted to exploit this access to gain further strategic access at the ministerial level. The background to this cyber incident was an incident published a week earlier in the first part of the report, which is also available here. In that incident, Secret Blizzard compromised the infrastructure of Pakistani threat actor Storm-0156 to gain access to Afghan government entities' computer systems.

column2: Cybercriminal group Storm-1919 and Russian state-sponsored actor Secret Blizzard (also known as Turla) deployed Tavdig and Kazuar backdoor on Ukrainian military devices beginning in March 2024

In that incident, Secret Blizzard compromised the infrastructure of Pakistani threat actor Storm-0156 to gain access to Afghan government entities' computer systems.

column2: Clop ransomware group exploited a zero-day vulnerability in Cleo's managed file transfer (MFT) software products LexiCom, VLTrader, and Harmony to breach corporate networks and steal data since at least 3 December 2024
column3:
A zero-day vulnerability in Cleo's managed file transfer (MFT) software products LexiCom, VLTrader, and Harmony has been actively exploited by hackers to breach corporate networks and steal data since at least 3 December 2024. The flaw allowed unrestricted file uploads and downloads, leading to remote code execution. This vulnerability (CVE-2024-50623) affected versions 5.8.0.21 and earlier and bypassed a previous patch released in October 2024.
Cybersecurity company Huntress wrote in its report of 9 December 2024 that at least ten organizations in industries like consumer goods, food, trucking, and shipping have been impacted, with the most vulnerable systems in the United States. Furthermore, Huntress wrote that the patch offered by Cleo does not work.
Cybersecurity expert Kevin Beaumont commented on the cyber incident on Mastodon on 10 December 2024 and said the Termite ransomware group had a zero-day exploit for those affected products.
The Clop ransomware group claimed responsibility for the cyber incident on 15 December 2024.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors gained access to the Spanish National Commission on Markets and Competition (CNMC) and exfiltrated approximately 240 GB of personal data of mobile phone line holders
column3:
On an unspecified date, unknown threat actors breached the confidentiality of the Spanish National Commission on Markets and Competition (CNMC) by gaining illegal access to their computer systems, exfiltrating approximately 240 GB of personal data of mobile phone line holders, representing about two billion records. The incident came to light because the Spanish international news agency EFE gained access to an order dated 5 December 2024, according to which the incident is to be transferred from the Court of Instruction 27 in Madrid to the National Court of Instruction 3. 
Judge Maria Tardon of the National accepted the competence to investigate the case, classified as a crime of computer attacks and against national security. The Office of the Public Prosecutor opposed this transfer because the CNMC cannot be described as a state institution. Maria Tardon objected, saying that a transfer must meet two criteria: first, the crime must have been committed against a high agency of the nation or a state institution, and second, the dignity or functioning of that institution must be affected.

column2: Unknown threat actors launched a ransomware attack against medical devices producer Artivion on 21 November 2024
column3:
On 21 November 2024 at the latest, unknown threat actors launched a ransomware attack on Artivion, a leading manufacturer of cardiac surgery devices. The attack encrypted some of its systems and stole data from compromised systems. The company took certain systems offline, initiated an investigation, and engaged external advisors to assess, contain, and remediate the incident. The attack disrupted Artivion's corporate operations, order processing, and shipping, but most disruptions have been addressed. 
Artivion disclosed the incident in a Monday 8-K filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) on 9 December 2024. Artivion explained that it is working to securely restore its systems as quickly as possible and evaluate any notification obligations. The company also believes it will incur additional costs not covered by insurance.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Launched a Ransomware Attack on Canadian Pembina Trails School Division on 2 December 2024
column3: On 2 December 2024, Pembina Trails School Division, located in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada, was hit by a ransomware attack, resulting in some of its systems, including phone lines, being taken offline. The division's network system was still down on the following day. Pembina Trails staff were informed of the attack on Tuesday, and the division was working on a fix. The specific nature of the cyber security incident and whether any personal data may have been exposed was not revealed. Outside experts were brought in to investigate, and the division was restoring interrupted services and investigating the incident. The division's information technology staff detected unusual activities in the network system on the morning of 2 December. The attack was reported by Supt. and CEO Shelley Amos in a letter to staff and parents, and later commented on by technology analyst and journalist Carmi Levi, who praised the division's quick response to the incident. Levi noted that the attack followed a depressingly familiar trajectory, reflecting a growing realization within the cybercriminal community that schools, school boards, and other public-facing institutions make for highly desirable targets. Levi also stated that the district will ultimately have to share specific guidance on what exactly happened, what's being done to recover from this event, and what steps will be taken to harden its systems, staff, and students against future attacks.

column2: The Pakistani-based threat actor Storm-0156 (also known as SideCopy or Transparent Tribe) compromised Afghan government networks beginning December 2022 at the latest
column3:
The Pakistani-based threat actor Storm-0156 (also known as SideCopy or Transparent Tribe) compromised Afghan government networks between December 2022 and mid-2023, using CrimsonRAT and Waiscot malware to gain access to these systems. This campaign focused on espionage, collecting sensitive information from Afghan government entities. Storm-0156's activities align with its broader objective of targeting South Asia's government, military, and industrial systems for intelligence.
Additionally, in another cyber incident, the Russian state-sponsored hacking group Turla infiltrated the Pakistani threat actor and used that access to infiltrate the Afghan government.

column2: Russian state-sponsored hacking group Turla (also known as Secret Blizzard) breached the infrastructure of Pakistani threat actor Storm-0156 and hijacked their command and control servers to gain access into already compromised networks of the Afghan government beginning in December 2022
column3: Between December 2022 and October 2024, the Russian state-sponsored hacking group Secret Blizzard (also known as Turla) breached the infrastructure of Pakistani threat actor Storm-0156. It hijacked their command and control servers to gain access to already compromised networks of the Afghan government. Turla deployed their malware tools, TwoDash and Statuezy. The attackers also targeted the Pakistani threat actors, gaining access to valuable data such as malware tools and stolen credentials and data. Lumen's Black Lotus Labs and Microsoft's Threat Intelligence Team tracked this campaign and reported that Turla's operation was primarily used to deploy backdoors on Afghan government entities. The attackers' approach allowed them to gather intelligence stealthily without exposing themselves or their toolset, shifting blame and complicating attribution efforts.

column2: China-linked threat actor MirrorFace delivered ANEL and NOOPDOOR backdoors to individuals and organizations in Japan via spear-phishing campaign in June 2024
column3: In June 2024, the China-linked threat actor known as MirrorFace launched a spear-phishing campaign targeting individuals and organizations in Japan, delivering backdoors known as NOOPDOOR and ANEL. The campaign used emails with links to Microsoft OneDrive, containing booby-trapped ZIP archives with themes related to interview requests and Japan’s economic security. The attackers used three infection vectors, including macro-enabled Word documents and Windows shortcut files, to deliver a malicious dropper called ROAMINGMOUSE, which acted as a dropper for ANEL and NOOPDOOR components. The backdoors were used to capture screenshots, upload/download files, load executables, and run commands via cmd.exe. Trend Micro reported the attack and provided technical analysis. The campaign was ongoing as of December 2024.

column2: Suspected Russian hackers stole e-mail adresses and login credentials of nearly 600 employees of the UK Ministry of Defence in November 2024
column3:
A significant cyberattack has targeted the UK Ministry of Defence (MoD), resulting in the theft and leak of nearly 600 employee passwords on the dark web. inews first reported on this cyber incident on 29 November 2024.
The breach, attributed to suspected Russian hackers, exposed sensitive information of military personnel, civilian staff, and defence contractors. The stolen credentials provided access to the Defence Gateway portal, a critical platform for communication, HR services, and health records, though it does not store classified information.
Affected employees are primarily UK-based, with others stationed overseas in Iraq, Qatar, Cyprus, and mainland Europe.
Initial investigations suggest hackers exploited vulnerabilities in personal devices used to access the platform, bypassing its multi-factor authentication system. Although no direct evidence links the attack to the Kremlin, the tools used indicate state-sponsored involvement, aligning with previous high-profile cyberattacks on Western institutions.
The MoD, in collaboration with the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC), is investigating and working to mitigate potential harm. This incident occurs amidst heightened UK-Russia tensions, underscoring growing concerns over cyberattacks and espionage activities targeting critical national infrastructure.

DBP, Inc. responded by disconnecting the compromised server from the internet, purchasing new hardware, and implementing enhanced security controls.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Crashed Dewan Farooque Motors Limited (DFML) IT Servers In Pakistan Using Malware In A Cyberattack On 28 November 2024
column3: On 28 November 2024, unknown threat actors carried out a malware cyberattack on Dewan Farooque Motors Limited (DFML), crashing the company's IT servers and corrupting its data. The attack led to the postponement of the DFML board of directors meeting, which was originally scheduled for 28 November 2024. The incident was disclosed by DFML in a stock filing on 30 November 2024, and reported by Dawn, a Pakistani English-language newspaper, on the same day. The company stated that the restoration of its information system and financial data, including that of the first quarter ending 30 September 2024, is necessary and will take considerable time.

column2: Russia-Aligned Cybercrime Group 'RomCom' Exploited A Zero-Day Vulnerability In Firefox In The United States At The Beginning Of October 2024
column3: The Russia-aligned cybercrime group 'RomCom' exploited two zero-day vulnerabilities, one in Mozilla Firefox and the other in Microsoft Windows, in November 2024, ESET disclosed in a technical report. The vulnerabilities in question are CVE-2024-9680, a use-after-free breach in the Firefox Animation component, and CVE-2024-49039, a vulnerability of privilege escalation in Windows Task Scheduler. The attackers used a fake website to redirect potential victims to a server hosting the malicious payload, which combined the two flaws to achieve code execution and release the RomCom RAT. The attack chain involved social engineering for the distribution of malicious URLs, and the exploit was activated if the site was visited by a vulnerable version of the Firefox browser. The attackers were able to execute arbitrary code without any interaction from the user, leading to the installation of the RomCom backdoor on the victim's computer. The attack was attributed to RomCom, also known as Storm-0978, Tropical Scorpius, UAC-0180, UNC2596, and Void Rabisu, which has a history of cybercrime and espionage operations dating back to at least 2022. The US government has recommended avoiding memory-unsafe languages to prevent this type of flaw. ESET telemetry data shows that most of the victims who visited the site hosting the exploit were in Europe and North America.

column2: Hacktivist group NoName057(16) Launched A DDoS Attack On The Coroners Court Of Victoria In Australia On 25 November 2024
column3:
The hacktivist group NoName057(16) conducted a denial-of-service attack targetinghe Coroners Court of Victoria on 25 November 2024. Threat researcher Dark Web Informer reported the incident, which was later confirmed by the Coroners Court of Victoria. A spokesperson for the court stated that while the attack disrupted its public website, there was no unauthorized access to sensitive systems, including case records, employee data, or financial information. Immediate actions were taken to mitigate the impact and restore service.
This incident is part of a broader campaign by NoName, a ransomware group implicated in global DDoS-for-hire schemes. .

column2: South East Asian cyber gang 'Waste' stole 16 million euros from the Bank of Uganda in mid-November 2024
column3:
In mid-November 2024, a cyber attack by South East Asian threat actors, who refer to themselves as 'Waste', breached the confidentiality of Uganda's central bank, 'Benki Kuu ya Uganda', by infiltrating the bank's IT infrastructure and transferring 62.4 billion Ugandan shillings, equivalent to around 16 million euros, from an account. The Ugandan newspaper Daily Monitor reported the attack on 28 November 2024. The central bank managed to recover more than half of the stolen amount. The police initially led the investigation but were later taken over by the country's secret services. An external company was also commissioned to carry out an investigation. President Yoweri Museveni was involved in the case. 
Uganda’s biggest independent newspaper, Daily Monitor, reported that the theft may have involved collusion by insiders. According to a report in the online Monitor, “security agencies are treating it as an ‘inside job’, that was the result of collusion between the Bank of Uganda, the Finance Ministry’s Treasury Department and the Accountant General’s Office.”

column2: Unknown threat actors launched a ransomware attack on Costa Rican Petroleum Refinery RECOPE in Costa Rica on 27 November 2024
column3: On 27 November 2024, the Costa Rican Petroleum Refinery RECOPE was targeted by unknown threat actors in a ransomware attack, forcing the institution to resort to manual fuel sales. The attack was detected in the early hours of the same day, prompting the activation of security protocols and an ongoing analysis of the attack's impact. RECOPE reported the incident in a press release on the same day, assuring customers that supply and service were unaffected. In collaboration with the Computer Security Incident Response Center of the Cybersecurity Directorate of the Ministry of Science, Innovation, Technology and Telecommunications of Costa Rica (Micitt), the institution's technical team is working to resolve the situation and strengthen security systems. RECOPE's president, Karla Montero, made a public call for calm while experts analyzed the attack's impact.The unknown perpetrators demanded a ransom of 5 million US dollars.

column2: Russian State-Sponsored Hacking Group TAG-110 Hacked Human Rights Groups, Private Security Companies, And Educational Institutions In Central Asia, East Asia, And Europe Between July 2024 And November 2024
column3: Between July 2024 and November 2024, Russian state-sponsored threat actors, including Unit 29155 and TAG-110, likely linked to the Russian cyber-espionage group APT28 (also known as Fancy Bear/APT28/Sofacy/Pawn Storm/Group 74/Sednit/Tsar Team/Forest Blizzard fka STRONTIUM/Grizzly Steppe/SNAKEMACKEREL/IRON TWILIGHT/TG-4127/Group G0007/ITG05/BlueDelta (GRU, 85th Main Special Service Center (GTsSS) Military Unit 26165)), carried out a cyber-espionage campaign targeting human rights groups, private security companies, and educational institutions in Central Asia, East Asia, and Europe. The attackers used custom malware, including the Hatvibe loader and the Cherryspy backdoor, delivered through malicious Microsoft Word email attachments, to infect at least 62 unique victims, primarily in Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan, Turkmenistan, and Kazakhstan. The campaign, which is believed to have been ongoing since at least 2021, aimed to gather insights into geopolitical events in neighbouring countries and reinforce Russia's military efforts in Ukraine. The attacks were attributed to TAG-110 by Recorded Futures Insikt Group.

column3: On 23 October 2024, the RansomHub gang breached the confidentiality and availability of the information systems of the City of Coppell, Texas with a ransomware attack. column2: Chinese state-sponsored hacking group Flax Typhoon infiltrated Paraguayan government networks, intercepting emails and accessing the email server beginning at least in April 2024
column3:
In 2024, the Chinese cyber-espionage group Flax Typhoon infiltrated Paraguayan government networks, intercepting emails and accessing the email server. The United States Embassy in Paraguay and the Ministry of Information and Communication Technologies (MITIC) publicly disclosed the incident on 26 November 2024.
The attack was first detected by the Ministry of Information and Communication Technologies (Mitic), after which the Paraguay government requested the help of U.S. experts. These details were given by the Director of Cybersecurity and Information Protection at MITIC, Jorge Levera,  in ABC Paraguay on 26 and 27 November 2024. He said that in April 2024, they noticed external traffic when the workplaces were not occupied. As a result, MITIC reportedly recognized that the e-mail server was accessed without authorization and found out in collaboration with the U.S. Southern Command that e-mails were intercepted in May and September 2024. In the media reports, Levera speaks of a front company in China that offers its services to other companies and the Chinese government. The attackers' source IP was traced back to China, and while the reasons behind the attack are still unknown, it is presumed that the company may have sold the data to the Chinese government.
Meanwhile, in its press release, the U.S. Embassy in Paraguay refers to Flax Typhoon.

column2: The Termite ransomware group disrupted the managed services hosted environment of supply chain management and software provider Blue Yonder since at least 21 November 2024
column3:
The Termite ransomware group disrupted the managed services hosted environment of supply chain management and software provider Blue Yonder since at least 21 November 2024, Blue Yonder reported in an incident notification for the first time on 22 November 2024.
The incident involved a ransomware attack, and at least at the coffee chain Starbucks, the incident disrupted the system they use to track and manage their employees' schedules. The retailer Wm Morrison Supermarkets was also affected. In December 2024, the Termite ransomware group claimed to have exfiltrated 680 GB of data, including databases, email lists "for future attacks" (over 16,000 entries), miscellaneous documents (over 200,000), reports, and insurance documents.
On January 29, 2025, Morrisons supermarkets reported that the cyberattack on its technology supplier, Blue Yonder, in November disrupted product availability and impacted Christmas sales. CEO Rami Baitieh explained that the attack forced the shutdown of the company’s warehouse management system, resulting in a temporary loss of visibility over fresh produce and fruit and vegetable stock levels.

column2: Russian state-sponsored hacking group GruesomeLarch (also known as APT 28) breached an unknown organization in the United States by exploiting neighboring Wi-Fi networks in February 2022
column3:
In 2022, Russian state-sponsored hackers, specifically Unit 26165 of the 85th Main Special Services Center, also known as APT 28, Forest Blizzard, Fancy Bear and GruesomeLarch, breached a Washington, D.C.-based organization, named Organization A in Volexity's report, focused on Ukraine by exploiting Wi-Fi connectivity, using a new attack technique dubbed a 'nearest neighbour attack'. The attackers initially attempted a password-spray attack against a public entry point but were hampered by a multi-factor authentication requirement. They then compromised at least two nearby organization's networks, named Organisations B and C in Volexity's report, using credentials originating from a password-spray attack in January and February 2022. Starting from these two organizations close to the actual target, they connected to the victim's Wi-Fi network, as the target's Wi-Fi system didn't require multi-factor authentication. Volexity detected the hacking campaign on 4 February 2022, when hackers began exfiltrating data through compressed files, using Microsoft's native Cipher.exe utility to evade detection. Following Volexity's initial comprehensive analysis and countermeasures at an unspecified point, Volexity discovered that the hackers could reaccess Organisation C's network approximately one month after the initial countermeasures.
The breach was first reported at the 2024 Cyberwarcon conference in Arlington, Virginia, and was disclosed by Steven Adair, a researcher and the president of Volexity and Ars Technica, on 22 November 2024. 
The attackers claimed no responsibility for the incident.

The institution has closed the extranet site affected by this cybercriminal visit.

The National Information Systems Directorate DNSI and the National Information Systems Security Agency ANSSI are working together to reestablish all services as soon as possible. A complaint has been lodged with the authorities. The incident coincided with another affecting the higher regional chamber of Occitane.

column3: On 21 November 2024, unknown hacktivists breached The Real World platform associated with Andrew Tate. Additionally, the primary chatroom was flooded with pro-feminist and LGBTQ emojis, temporarily banning users, and deleting attachments. The incident was first reported by DailyDot and DDoSecrets.

column2: Hacker collective Lynx launched a ransomware attack on INPS Servizi S.p.A. in Italy beginning 18 November 2024
column3: On 18 November 2024, a INPS Servizi S.p.A., a subsidary of the Italian National Institute of Social Security INPS, suffered a ransomware attack, breaching its computer security and encrypting sensitive data. The attack is suspected by experts to be conducted by ransomware group Lynx, an apparent re-organisation of INC Ransomware group. The incident had no impact on the computer systems of the INPS, but the website of INPS Servizi as well as management applications provided to the INPS were inaccessible at time of reporting. However, the Contact Center, the main service provided by INPS Servizi, was not hit by the attack and remains operational. Following the incident, INPS is providing active technical and advisory support to facilitate the rapid restoration of the IT systems of INPS Servizi SPA.

column2: Unknown threat actors hacked Palo Alto Networks firewalls in the United States using zero-day vulnerabilities in November 2024
column3: Over 2,000 Palo Alto Networks firewalls were hacked in November 2024, exploiting recently patched zero-day vulnerabilities CVE-2024-0012 and CVE-2024-9474 in PAN-OS. The attackers gained administrator privileges and deployed web shells on compromised devices, granting persistent remote access. Palo Alto Networks and their Unit 42 tracked the exploitation activity, and Shadowserver researchers reported that approximately 2,000 devices were hacked, primarily in the US (554) and India (461), followed by Thailand (80), Mexico (48), Indonesia (43), Turkey (41), the U.K. (39), Peru (36), and South Africa (35). The incident was publicly disclosed by Palo Alto Networks and Unit 42. IP addresses 136.144.17.173, 239.218.251, and 216.73.162 were identified as being associated with the malicious activities. Palo Alto Networks initially warned customers on 8 November about restricting access to their next-generation firewalls due to an unspecified remote code execution flaw, and the company has observed a notable increase in threat activity following the public release of technical insights by third-party researchers on November 19, 2024. The attackers' identities and motivations remain yet unknown.

column2: Threat actor named near2tlg breached at least one customer account on digital patient record system Mediboard and stole sensitive personal data of 758,912 French individuals
column3: On 19 November 2024, the threat actor named near2tlg claimed to have breached the confidentiality of at least one customer account on the digital patient record system Mediboard by usurping a privileged account. Mediboard is an open-source web application used by many healthcare institutions. The threat actor stole sensitive personal data of 758,912 French individuals, including names, dates of birth, postal and e-mail addresses, telephone numbers, medical prescriptions, physician information, death declarations, mutual card histories, and external identifiers. The hacker claimed the data leak on the BreachForums hack site and published an excerpt. Softway Medical Group, the developer of Mediboard, stated that the breach was not related to Mediboard itself but rather a result of compromised identifiers used by a person to access the system of one of Mediboard's customers. The Softway Medical Group did not host the health data concerned.

column3: On 19 November 2024, unnamed South Korean drone research and development institutes were targeted by a ransomware attack. The attackers sent a private email with a malicious file attachment to South Korean drone research and development companies, universities and institutions. The email bypassed the defence systems of some small businesses, resulting in the infection of classified PCs and USB software. The National Intelligence Service (NIS) is investigating the attack and checking the servers of companies and research agencies to determine the extent of the damage.

column2: RansomHub ransomware gang exfiltrated 313GB of data from the Mexican Government in November 2024
column3:
On 15 November 2024, the RansomHub ransomware gang, claimed to have breached the Mexican government's official website, specifically targeting the Legal Counsel of the Federal Executive Branch CJEF, and exfiltrated 313GB of confidential information, including contracts, administrative information, financial data, emails, and personal details of federal officials, which was later posted as a sample on their website, with a ransom deadline of 25 November 2024. On 20 November 2024, Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum confirmed that her government is investigating the alleged cyberattack and is awaiting a detailed report.
The cybercriminals RansomHub removed the 206 gigabytes of confidential information belonging to the Mexican Presidency's legal counsel that had been published on the dark web on 28 November 2024. According to Manuel Rivera, director of the cybersecurity company Nekt Group, the attack was supported by local partners who could be employed or ex-employed by the Department itself.

column2: Embargo ransomware group launched a ransomware attack on American Associated Pharmacies (AAP) in the United States
column3: EMBARGO ransomware group launched a ransomware attack on American Associated Pharmacies (AAP), a U.S. healthcare organization managing over 2,000 pharmacies across the U.S. The attackers allegedly stole 1.469 TB of AAP's data, scrambled AAP's files, and demanded a ransom payment. AAP reportedly paid the cybercriminals $1.3 million for decryption and now faces another $1.3 million demand to prevent its data exposure. The EMBARGO ransomware group uses endpoint detection and response (EDR) killing tools and a Rust-based ransomware kit. Check Point and the Register reported the attack. AAP posted a statement on its website about the incident, informing users that all passwords associated with APIRx.com and RxAAP.com had been reset, and limited ordering capabilities for API Warehouse had been restored at APIRx.com.

column2: Unknown threat actors launched a ransomware attack on Great Plains Regional Medical Center in the United States beginning on 5 September 2024
column3: On September 5-8, 2024, unknown threat actors launched a ransomware attack on Great Plains Regional Medical Center in Oklahoma, compromising personal data of 133,000 individuals, including sensitive information such as name, demographic information, health insurance information, clinical treatment information, drivers license number, and/or Social Security number. The attackers accessed and encrypted files on the systems and copied some of them. The incident was reported to the US Department of Health and Human Services by the Oklahoma Medical Center. The organization quickly restored its systems and returned to normal operations, but a limited amount of patient information was not recoverable. No ransomware groups claimed responsibility for the security breach. The incident was publicly disclosed by the organization through a notice of security incident on their website and reported to the US Department of Health and Human Services on 19 November 2024.

column2: The hacker known as abyss0 allegedly stole a large amount of presumably customer data from financial technology company Finastra
column3: On 7 November 2024, Finastra detected suspicious activity on its internally hosted file transfer platform, which led to the discovery of a large-scale data breach. Using the nickname 'abyss0', an unknown threat actor had stolen over 400 gigabytes of data, including customer information, and began selling it on the cybercrime community BreachForums on 8 November 2024. This actor also tried to sell the stolen data before, on 31 October 2024. Therefore, the breach is believed to have occurred at least a week before detection, with the threat actor gaining access to Finastra's file-sharing system through compromised credentials. Finastra notified its customers, including 45 of the world's top 50 banks, and implemented an alternative secure file-sharing platform to ensure continuity. The company is still investigating the root cause and scope of the breach and has been sharing Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) and updates with its customers. KrebsOnSecurity reported the incident on 19 November 2024.

column2: Unknown threat actors breached French retailer Auchan in France, exposing hundreds of thousands of customers personal data
column3:
Unknown threat actors compromised French retailer Auchan, resulting in unauthorized access to the personal data of approximately 500,000 customers, including surname, first name, email, postal address, telephone number, date of birth, loyalty card number, and family composition, but not banking data, passwords, or PIN codes. Auchan reported the incident to the National Commission on Informatics and Freedoms (Cnil) and took measures to stop the attack and strengthen its information systems. The company warned customers to be vigilant against phishing campaigns and false business advisors.
Auchan’s hypermarket in Roncq is very popular with Belgian customers as it is very close to the border, so it is to be expected that a lot of Belgian customers may also have had their data stolen.

column2: Algerian hackers launched a DDoS attack on Morocco Hebdo website on 16 November 2024
column3: On 16 November 2024, Morocco Hebdo, a Moroccan web platform, was targeted by a Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) cyber attack, allegedly carried out by Algerian threat actors, in an attempt to silence the platform. The attack occurred after the publication of a cover file on the increasingly belligerent gestures of the Algerian regime against Morocco. Morocco Hebdo reported the incident in a press release on 18 November 2024, stating that their technical teams had established the attack's origin as coming from Algeria. The attackers aimed to bring down the website by overwhelming it with requests in a short period. Morocco Hebdo disclosed the attack and attributed it to Algerian threat actors.

column2: Unknown threat actors stole $12 million worth of cryptocurrencies from Polter Finance in Singapore on November 17th, 2024
column3: On 17 November 2024, unknown threat actors carried out a flash loan attack on Polter Finance, a decentralized loan-and-receiving platform, resulting in a $12 million loss. The attack was linked to an accidental price error in the SpookySwap BOO market, which Polter Finance had just launched. The platform's operations were halted, and the price of POLTER dropped by 86% from $0.125 to $0.0171. Polter Finance's anonymous founder reported the attack to the Singapore police on the same day and confirmed his identity using a digital identity system called Singpass. The founder claimed that the attack resulted in the theft of $12 million worth of crypto assets. Polter Finance established a partnership with the Security Alliance Information Sharing and Analysis Centre (SEAL-ISAC) to track down the attacker and is working to recover the stolen funds.

column2: An Unnamed Hacker Exploited A Vulnerability And Withdrew A Total Of $25.5 Million Worth Of Tokens From The Thala DeFi Platform On 15 November 2024
column3:
An undisclosed hacker exploited a vulnerability in the Thala DeFi platform on 15 November 2024 and withdrew tokens with a total value of 25.5 million dollars. Thala, a decentralized finance protocol on the Aptos blockchain, was quickly able to identify the attacker, negotiate a bounty and recover the assets that had been frozen in the meantime. 
The protocol's codebase is currently undergoing a comprehensive review and re-audit of all affected and related packages, and Thala said in a statement that it will return all stolen funds to user accounts once it has re-audited its codebase.

column2: Unknown threat actors breached and exfiltrated sensitive data from JewishCare NSW in Australia
column3: JewishCare NSW, a healthcare provider for the Australian Jewish community, suffered a comprehensive data breach, resulting in the exfiltration of sensitive data, including personal and financial information, of clients, staff, volunteers, donors, and suppliers. JewishCare discovered the data breach on 28 October, which may have been posted on the dark web. The breach affected various data types, including birth dates, phone numbers, email addresses, residential and postal addresses, bank account information, credit card details, identity documents, health and medical data, and more. JewishCare has notified affected individuals and is working with federal and state authorities, including the Australian Cyber Security Centre, the Australian Federal Police, NSW Police, the National Office of Cyber Security, and the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner, to investigate the incident. The threat actor behind the cyber attack is currently unknown.

column2: Prorussian hacking group Indohaxsec based in Indonesia gained access to the network of the Federal Statistical Office of Germany and offered to sell stolen data for 250 USD on the darknet
column3: A cyber attack was carried out by the Indonesian Cyber Attack (Indohaxsec) group, which supports Russia, targeting the German Federal Statistical Office (Destatis). The attackers presumably breached the system used for data exchange between Destatis and German companies, stealing sensitive data, including names, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses, and login credentials of companies that exchange statistical data with Destatis. The stolen data was offered for sale on the darknet for $250. The attackers claimed responsibility for the attack and published a preview of the stolen data, including contact information and several companies' login credentials. The attack is believed to have occurred despite the system being updated just a few months prior. The affected companies were not informed about the data breach at the time of publication. Indohaxsec had previously announced on Telegram that they would target Germany and had collaborated with the pro-Russian hacker group NoName05716.

column2: Chinese state-sponsored TAG-112 compromised Tibetan websites Tibet Post and Gyudmed Tantric University beginning on or around 23 May 2024
column3:
On or around 23 May 2024, Chinese state-sponsored hacking group TAG-112 compromised two websites linked to the Tibetan community, Tibet Post and Gyudmed Tantric University, by inducing visitors to download a malicious executable file disguised as a security certificate, which loads Cobalt Strike Beacon malware to the user's computer. 
The attack, reported by Insikt Group on 12 November 2024, was likely aimed at gathering information and surveillance rather than destructive attacks. Insikt Group informed the university and the news website of the hack, and Gyudmed Tantric University has remedied the problem starting this week, while the news website remained compromised.
Recorded Future attributed TAG-112 as a subgroup of the well-known Chinese state-sponsored proxy TAG-102, also known as Evasive Panda.

column2: Unknown threat actors breached the City of Sheboygan network in the United States  on or around November 1, 2024
column3: On or around 1 November 2024, unknown threat actors breached the confidentiality and integrity of the City of Sheboygan's network through an external, unauthorized access. The attackers left a ransom message. The City of Sheboygan secured the network and started a forensic review. Local cybersecurity expert Brian Collins, co-founder of PanaTech, commented on the attack, stating that the city likely lacked active monitoring of network activity and that it is harder to identify when someone has breached firewalls nowadays. The city stated that it had not discovered evidence that any sensitive personal information has been compromised by this incident. Public safety services were responding to all emergent and nonemergent requests for service with limited interruptions, and city employees with internet access could communicate with each other online as all cloud-based services were up and working. The incident was reported by TMJ4 News on 11 November 2024.

column2: Iranian state-sponsored hacking group Handala leaked personal details and documents of senior Israeli officials in November 2024
column3:

The Israeli daily Haaretz reported on this on 11 November 2024 and assumed, at least in the case of the Israeli nuclear scientist, that the relevant data was stolen from his phone or email rather than from the Soreq Nuclear Research Center.

column2: Unknown threat actors launched a ransomware attack on the Houston Housing Authority in the United States on 21 September 2024, encrypting the agency's virtual servers
column3: Unknown Threat Actors launched a ransomware attack on the Houston Housing Authority (HHA) on 21 September 2024, encrypting the agency's virtual servers. Although the HHA's firewall initially appeared to have thwarted data theft, the hackers later claimed to have obtained sensitive data from the agency. The HHA's IT director, Roy Spivey, and board chair, Jody Proler, assessed the agency's security vulnerabilities and formed an action plan, which included a $311,000 expenditure to bolster network security, adding two-factor authentication on all desktop servers, enhanced firewalls, more robust file recovery, and continuous monitoring across the agency's systems. The HHA plans to notify residents, staff, and vendors about the breach as it proceeds with its heightened cybersecurity efforts.

column2: Intel Broker leaked personal data of 290,762 individuals from MIT Technology Review Magazine in the United States via a third-party contractor on 4 November 2024
column3: The hacker known as Intel Broker breached MIT Technology Review Magazine via a third-party contractor, leaking nearly 300,000 user records on Breach Forums on 4 November 2024. The data includes full names, email addresses, and activity details, posing risks for phishing and targeted scams. Intel Broker, notorious for recent attacks on high-profile organizations, alleges that the data includes Personally Identifiable Information. Hackread.com analyzed the leaked data and can confirm it includes personal details, which, while not as sensitive as financial data, could be leveraged in phishing attempts or targeted scams. The leak does not appear to contain highly sensitive information like social security numbers or financial data, but the compromised data is still a privacy risk, as it could be exploited for phishing scams or identity verification attacks. MIT Technology Review, an esteemed publication from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, is known for its analysis of emerging technologies and their impact on society. This data breach potentially exposes readers and contributors who engaged with the platform, a situation that may lead to reputational challenges for the publication, as well as privacy concerns for its user base. Hackread.com disclosed the attack on 4 November 2024.

column2: The Hellcat ransomware gang compromised Schneider Electric in France using Hellcat ransomware
column3: The Hellcat ransomware gang compromised Schneider Electric, a multinational energy management company. A likely member of the ransomware group, who goes by the name 'Grep' or 'greppy', disclosed on X on 3 November 2024 gaining unauthorized access to one of Schneider Electric's internal project execution tracking platforms hosted within an isolated environment by using openly accessible login credentials and a MiniOrange REST-API. The attackers claimed to have over 40 gigabytes of data from the company's JIRA platform, including projects, issues, and plugins, along with over 400,000 rows of user data, totalling 75,000 unique email addresses and the names of Schneider Electric employees and customers. The attackers demanded a $125,000 ransom in Monero. Schneider Electric reported the incident, which marks the third time in the past 18 months that ransomware groups attacked the company, following incidents in January 2024 and June 2023. Schneider Electric disclosed the incident on 5 November 2024.

column2: Chinese state-sponsored hacking group Volt Typhoon compromised Singaporean telecom provider Singapore Telecommunications (SingTel) in June 2024
column3:
Chinese state-sponsored hacking group Volt Typhoon compromised Singaporean telecom provider Singapore Telecommunications (SingTel) in June 2024.
Bloomberg reported this on November 5, 2024, based on two unnamed people involved in the matter. One of these people also attributed the cyber incident to the Chinese state-sponsored hacking group Volt Typhoon.
According to the statements, the Singaporean telecommunications provider Singapore Telecommunications (SingTel) discovered suspicious data traffic in a specific end router and, therefore, malware in the network. 
The sources assumed that the cyber incident against SingTel could be considered an initial attempt at other cyber incidents, probably in the United States, or as a starting point for future cyber incidents, also probably against targets in the United States.
In June 2024, a cyber incident occurred, with the Chinese state-sponsored hacking group Salt Typhoon attacking many US telecommunications providers.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Accessed and Modified Bank Information from Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) in Canada Tax Accounts Using Identifiers from H&R Block Canada, Filing Hundreds of False Statements and Diverting $6 Million, Between 2020 and 2023
column3: Unknown threat actors compromised sensitive information from the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) during the 2024 tax season, affecting tens of thousands of Canadians. Exploiting client identifiers linked to H&R Block Canada, the attackers accessed CRA tax accounts, modified bank details, filed hundreds of false statements, and diverted $6 million before detection. The CRA’s investigation revealed suspicious repayment patterns, including refunds to shared bank accounts and centralized mailing addresses. Internal investigations by CRA and H&R Block found no breaches in their systems, attributing the increase in attacks to emergency COVID-19 benefits introduced in 2020. With over 31,000 privacy violations and $190 million in suspected fraudulent payments, the CRA has since bolstered security measures, offering credit protection solutions to victims. The incident was publicly disclosed through a joint Radio-Canada and CBC investigation on 31 October 2024.

While not all attacks were confirmed by the relevant authorities, the Portsmouth City Council and BCP Council confirmed that their websites were disrupted by the attack, with other councils also experiencing temporary outages as they worked to take systems offline. The National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) provided guidance to the affected councils, and no user or resident data were reported to be at risk.

column2: North Korean State-Sponsored Hacking Group Andariel Compromised An Unnamed Organisation Between May And September 2024 Before Play Ransomware Was Deployed Against The Organisation
column3:
According to a 30 October 2024 report by Unit42, North Korean state-sponsored hacking group Andariel (aka Jumpy Pisces, Onyx Sleet, fka PLUTONIUM/Silent Chollima/G0138/DarkSeoul) infiltrated an unnamed target in May 2024, leading to persistent access and data theft by the group from May to September 2024. Following the theft of data, Unit42 indicates a potential collaboration with the Play ransomware group, since Play ransomware was deployed against the target at the end of September 2024. 
In late May 2024, Andariel compromised the unnamed organisation's network via a compromised user account, and then extracted registry dumps and deployed Mimikatz for credential harvesting. The threat actors solidified their presence on the network over the next few months, establishing command-and-control, stealing data, creating malicious services, establishing Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) sessions, and uninstalling endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools. In early September 2024, an unknown actor gained access to the same compromised user account and carried out "pre-ransomware activities" (credential harvesting, privilege escalation, etc.) according to Unit42. By the end of the month, Play ransomware was deployed onto the victim's servers.  
Unit42 links Andariel to Play with medium confidence due to the fact that Andariel had access to the account up until the day before the deployment of Play ransomware. However, Unit42 also states that Andariel could have simply sold access to the compromised user account to Play, rather than coordinate with the group directly.

column2: Unknown Hackers Infiltrated the Systems of Landmark Admin and Stole Data from Over 800,000 Individuals in May and June 2024
column3: On 23 October 2024, Landmark Admin, LLC (“Landmark Admin”) filed a notice of data breach with the Attorney General of Maine after discovering that hackers were able to encrypt and remove data from Landmark Admin’s system. One of the affected customers is the Liberty Bankers Insurance Group, which data is included in the breach. In this notice, Landmark Admin explains that the incident resulted in an unauthorized party being able to access consumers’ sensitive information, which includes their names; addresses; Social Security numbers; drivers’ license numbers; state identification card numbers; passport numbers; bank account and routing numbers; medical information; health insurance policy numbers; dates of birth; and life and annuity policy information. Upon completing its investigation, Landmark Admin began sending out data breach notification letters to all 806,519 individuals whose information was affected by the recent data security incident. On 13 May 2024, Landmark Admin detected suspicious activity within its IT network. The following day, Landmark Admin determined that an unauthorized party had attempted to access its network and was able to regain access to its IT network on 17 June 2024, including certain files containing confidential consumer information. Thus, the unauthorized activity on Landmark Admin’s systems occurred between 13 May 2024 and 17 June 2024.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Attacked Seven Secondary Schools in the District of Kitzingen, Germany, by Breaching Their IT Systems on 23 October 2024
column3:
Unknown threat actors attacked seven secondary schools in the district of Kitzingen on Wednesday, 23 October 2024, by breaching the IT systems of the state schools in Kitzingen and Dettelbach, the Armin-Knab-Gymnasium in Kitzingen, the Gymnasium Marktbreit, as well as the vocational school, the FOS/BOS and the Erich-Kästner-Schule in Kitzingen, causing partial encryption of the IT systems by ransomware, with no signs of data exfiltration initially, but a possible breach of data protection reported by the schools, with the Landratsamt Kitzingen disclosing the incident on 25 October 2024, and cooperating closely with the criminal police of Würzburg, the Commissioner for Cybercrime and the Bavarian State Office for Information Security to deal with the incident, and the Bavarian State Ministry of Education and Culture having established a security framework for IT security in schools.
The attack was also reported by the media on 6 November 2024, with the Landratsamt Kitzingen confirming that the attackers had stolen data from one of the schools, and that the data was personal data, with the victims to be informed as soon as the analyses of the data have been completed, and no publication of the data could be established as before.
