column2: The Italian Private Investigation Company Equalize Gained Access to the Databases of the Italian Law Enforcement Agencies, the Italian Revenue Agency, and the Bank of Italy and Stole Data Beginning in 2019
column3:
The Italian private investigation company Equalize gained access to the databases of the Italian law enforcement agencies, the Italian Revenue Agency, and the Bank of Italy and stole data beginning in 2019, reported the Milanese public prosecutor, Francesco de Tommasi, in the course of the investigation's disclosure on 25 October 2024.
The Milan, Bari and Perugia public prosecutor's offices were involved in the investigation. They brought charges against around 60 people for membership in a criminal organisation to abuse access to a computer system, corruption, disclosure of official secrets, abusive wiretapping and aiding and abetting.
The focus is on the private investigation company Equalize and its circle, which is said to have consisted of hackers, computer consultants, private intelligence agencies and police officers. The managing director of Equalize and former police officer Carmine Gallo; the chairman of the Fondazione Fiera Milano, Enrico Pazzali; Gallo's business partner, Nunzio Samuele Calamucci; and the owners or members of associated companies, Massimiliano Camponoro and Giulio Cornelli, were placed under house arrest. Carmine Gallo was wiretapped for having contacts with the mafia and for having 800,000 entries of the database of the Italian law enforcement agencies, SDI. 
The stolen data was primarily intended to be used to harm competitors or political opponents of Pazzali or people associated with him. Furthermore, the stolen data was sold to clients with the same interest, including large companies and professional and law firms.

column2: Unknown Threat Actor Targets Various Sectors in Hong Kong and Pakistan in Espionage Campaign "Operation Cobalt Whisper" Since May 2024
column3:
According to the Indian software company Seqrite, an unspecified threat actor targets various sectors in espionage campaign "Operation Cobalt Whisper" since May 2024. The new campaign targets the defence and research sectors in South Asia, particularly Pakistan and Hong Kong, but with growing interest in India. The focus is on engineering researchers and professors, using sophisticated lures like decoy documents related to electrotechnical societies and energy infrastructure. The campaign employs the post-exploitation tool Cobalt Strike, indicating a methodical approach to cyber-espionage. Seqrite further lists the following victims: 
Defense Industry, Electrotechnical Engineering, Energy (Hydropower, Renewable Energy), Civil Aviation, Environmental Engineering, Academia and Research Institutions, Medical Science Institutions, Cybersecurity Researchers.

column2: Slovenian University of Maribor Hit by Ransomware on 23 October 2024
column3:

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Breached French Telecom Free’s Data Management Systems in October 2024
column3:
Unknown threat actors infiltrated the management tools of French telecom provider Free, accessing personal data associated with certain subscriber accounts. While Free reported that no passwords, bank card details, or communications content were compromised, the precise scope and timing of the breach remain undisclosed. In response, Free has lodged a criminal complaint and alerted the National Commission for Information Technology and Civil Liberties (CNIL) and the National Agency for the Security of Information Systems (ANSSI).
On October 21, 2024, an unnamed user on BreachForums put the stolen data from Free up for sale. Although no bank card details were released, over 5 million International Bank Account Numbers (IBANs) were allegedly stolen as part of the attack, with the hackers releasing a sample (100,000) of these numbers on BreachForums.
The Paris judicial court has ordered Telegram to reveal the identity of a hacker behind a ransomware attack on October 31. Free and Free Mobile reported that a cybercriminal identified as "[Z] [L]" sent three messages on October 21 through their internal platform for personal data protection, followed by a fourth message addressed to the "president of the Iliad group," Xavier Niel, via Telegram. The hacker demanded a ransom of €10 million in cryptocurrencies. Telegram has been given 48 hours to provide identifying information, such as IP addresses, the phone number used to create the account, and civil identity details.
On 6 December 2024, cybersecurity researcher Clément Domingo (also known as SaxX on X) uncovered that an internal agent likely provided their OpenVPN credentials to a third party, potentially the hacker. That way, the hacker leveraged stolen VPN credentials to infiltrate Free's secure network. Masquerading as IT personnel, the attacker manipulated other employees through social engineering to gain additional credentials, enabling the hacker to access vast customer data. This leak is said to have been known among certain employees, but none of them dared to disclose it to their superiors.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Compromised World Uyghur Congress Staff Member’s Email to Spread Misinformation on 21 October 2024
column3: Unknown threat actors, believed to be affiliated with the Chinese government, compromised the email account of a World Uyghur Congress (WUC) staff member on 21 October 2024, and sent a notification to all registered attendees of the WUC general assembly in Sarajevo, falsely claiming that the event was postponed. The WUC released copies of the forged emails on its social media accounts to notify attendees that the information was incorrect. The incident occurred ahead of the 24-27 October general assembly of the WUC, an international organization of the ethnic group's diaspora from more than 25 countries in Central Asia, the Middle East, Europe, and North America. While the Chinese Embassy in Sarajevo firmly denies any allegations regarding their involvement in the incident.

column2: Inc Ransom Compromised Data of Nearly 800,000 Patients in OnePoint Patient Care Breach Between 6 And 8 August 2024
column3: Criminal actors compromised the systems of US-based OnePoint Patient Care, a pharmacy specializing in hospice and palliative care, exposing the personal data of 795,916 individuals between 6 and 8 August 2024. The breach affected names, addresses, medical records, diagnoses, prescriptions, and, for some, Social Security numbers. OnePoint detected suspicious network activity on 8 August, initiating an investigation and containment measures with forensic experts. The incident, while not affecting operations, was later linked to the Inc Ransom ransomware group, which leaked stolen data after the company declined to pay the ransom. Impacted individuals have been advised to monitor credit and financial activity for suspicious transactions. OnePoint notified their customers on 21 October 2024.

column2: Hackers Exploited a FortiManager Vulnerability and Compromised Associated FortiManager Devices Since at Least June 2024
column3:
Various sources reported that Hackers have used the vulnerability (CVE-2024-47575) to compromise FortiManager devices since at least 23 September 2024.
A possible vulnerability in FortiManager first became public in a Reddit conversation on 19 October 2024, after Fortinet may have privately notified these individuals.
On 23 October, cybersecurity researcher Kevin Beaumont published a post on the Medium website naming a vulnerability in the FortiManager protocol. In the same post, Beaumont criticized Fortinet's reluctance to disclose the vulnerability and attributed the exploitation to nation-state actors.
The following day, 24 October 2024, Fortinet published the vulnerability notification for the first time. On the same day, Mandiant also published its technical report and wrote that over 50 devices had been compromised since at least 27 June 2024. The vulnerability allowed a compromised FortiManager device to issue arbitrary commands. Mandiant attributed this incident to the group UNC5820, which exfiltrated the configuration data of the FortiGate devices managed by the compromised FortiManager.

column2: The Hacking Group TA866 (Also Known as Asylum Ambuscade) Gained Access to the Networks of Private and Public Targets in Various Countries Beginning in 2022
column3:
According to a report by IT security firm Cisco Talos Intelligence, on 23 October 2024, the hacking group TA866 (also known as Asylum Ambuscade) gained access to the networks of private and public targets in various countries beginning in 2022.
The hacking group gained initial access via malicious spam or advertising in 2022 and 2023. It also used a variety of malware, including WasabiSeed, ScreenShotter and AHK Bot, as well as the persistent backdoors Resident, CSharp- Streamer RAT, Cobalt Strike, and Rhadamanthys in 2023. In early 2024, the hacking group used Cobalt Strike and CSharp-Streamer RAT in WarmCookie/Bad 
The targets included manufacturing, government, and financial services, mainly in the United States but also in Canada, Germany, the United Kingdom, Italy, Austria, and the Netherlands.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Launched a Ransomware Attack on Schaffhauser Berufsbildungszentrum (BBZ) in Switzerland from 2 October 2024 to 21 October 2024
column3: Unknown threat actors launched a ransomware attack on the Schaffhauser Berufsbildungszentrum (BBZ) in Switzerland on 2 October 2024, exploiting a security vulnerability in the firewall to block access to several IT systems and demand a ransom. The attack was discovered on 3 October, and the school, in collaboration with internal and external experts and the police, took technical measures to protect the IT infrastructure from further attacks. The incident was reported to the Schaffhauser Polizei, the Bundesamt für Cybersicherheit, and the kantonalen Datenschutzbeauftragten. The school's IT systems are currently being restored, and an analysis of the attack is ongoing to determine the extent of the damage and develop measures to prevent future cyber attacks. The incident did not affect the IT systems of the Kanton Schaffhausen. The school informed employees, parents, students, and partner companies about the cyber attack, and the school's operations, including classes, resumed normally on 21 October 2024, after the autumn break.

column2: LulzSec Black Attempted to Breach Multiple Targets in Cyprus, Including Government Services and Private Companies, Using a Cyberattack Between 17 and 21 October 2024
column3: Between 17 and 21 October 2024, LulzSec Black, a hacking group, allegedly carried out six cyberattacks targeting government services and private companies in Cyprus, including the government’s main website, the Electricity Authority of Cyprus, Bank of Cyprus, telecommunication company Cyta, oil company EKO, and Hermes Airports, with the initial attempt on Hermes Airports' website being averted on 17 October, according to communications director Maria Kouroupi. The attacks were reportedly politically motivated, citing Cyprus' relationship with Israel as a factor. The cybersecurity firm Odyssey had warned of the imminent attack earlier in the week, stating that the group 'Moroccan Soldiers' had expressed their support for LulzSec Black in these operations. The targeted systems would have included Cypriot infrastructure, specifically government, financial, and critical services, which may have been at risk of service disruptions, data theft, or system compromises. The attacks caused disruptions at Larnaca airport, with long queues forming, while Paphos airport was less affected. The government’s main website was offline for a few minutes on Sunday, but was restored. Authorities, including police spokesman Christos Andreou and Deputy Minister Research Nicodemos Damianou, reported that the situation has normalised, and upgrades to control systems are being carried out. The Commissioner of Communications and head of the Digital Security Authority George Michaelides did not confirm whether there had been attempts to steal data and said the hackers’ motives and targets were not yet discussed. Cybersecurity experts, including Eleftherios Antoniades, Dinos Pastos, and Yianna Danidou, believe the attacks were a 'show of force' and a possible precursor to more significant attacks in the future, highlighting the need for improved cybersecurity measures in Cyprus.

column2: Pro-Russian NoName057(16) Threat Actors Launched DDoS Attacks on Japanese Public and Private Organisations from 15 to 17 October 2024
column3: Pro-Russian hacker group NoName057(16) launched a series of high-impact DDoS attacks against Japanese organizations, transportation, private companies, and government entities from 15-17 October 2024, in response to Japan's increasing militarization and its involvement in US-led military exercises. The Minister's Security Security Center is collecting information to prevent further damage, and the country's counter-cyber crime agencies have launched an investigation into the breach.

column2: IntelBroker, Together with Hacking Groups EnergyWeaponUser and zjj, Breached the Systems of Cisco on 6 October 2024 and Exfiltrated Customer Data
column3:
Unknown threat actors, allegedly IntelBroker, EnergyWeaponUser and zjj, breached the systems of Cisco, a networking hardware and software manufacturer, on 6 October 2024, and exfiltrated data belonging to it and its customers, including Github projects, Gitlab Projects, SonarQube projects, source code, hard-coded credentials, certificates, customer SRCs, Cisco confidential documents, Jira tickets, API tokens, AWS Private buckets, Cisco Technology SRCs, Docker Builds, Azure Storage buckets, Private & Public keys, SSL Certificates, and Cisco Premium Products, as well as the production source code of a large number of organisations, including Vodafone Australia, National Australia Bank (NAB) and Microsoft, according to claims posted by IntelBroker on a popular dark web hacking forum, which Cisco has acknowledged and is currently investigating. The attackers gained access to a DevHub environment, which was publicly accessible and contained software code and scripts, among other things, for customers. The instance has since been taken offline. Cisco has confirmed that data was leaked that was not intended for public download, but states that this does not include any financial data. The investigation is still ongoing, and the network equipment provider states that it is proactively approaching affected customers.
On 16 December 2024, IntelBroker leaked 2.9 GB of data belonging to Cisco on the Breach Forums platform. The leaked data included sensitive information from Cisco products and services, such as Cisco ISE (Identity Services Engine), Cisco SASE (Secure Access Service Edge), Cisco Webex, Cisco Umbrella, Cisco IOS XE & XR, and a Wireless LAN Controller software image. IntelBroker claimed this partial leak was intended to prove the legitimacy of the breach to potential buyers. The fate of the remaining 4.5 TB of data remains uncertain.

column2: The BianLian Ransomware Group Gained Access to Boston Children's Health Physicians (BCHP) and Stole Certain Files Beginning at Least on 10 September 2024
column3:
The BianLian ransomware group claimed on 16 October 2024, that it gained access to Boston Children's Health Physicians (BCHP) and stole certain files starting at least on 10 September 2024.
The BCHP incident notification stated that an unnamed BCHP IT provider detected suspicious activity on its network and subsequently informed the BCHP on 6 September 2024. On 10 September 2024, the BCHP also discovered unauthorized activity in limited portions of the network. Working with an IT security firm, the unauthorized access on 10 September 2024, was confirmed, as was the extraction of certain files.
The stolen files included current and former employee, patient, and guarantor information such as names, Social Security numbers, addresses, dates of birth, driver's license numbers, medical record numbers, health insurance information, billing information, and/or limited treatment information.

column2: Crypt Ghouls Launched Ransomware Attacks on Russian Energy, Mining, Finance, and Retail Companies Using LockBit 3.0 and Babuk in October 2024
column3: The hacker group 'Crypt Ghouls' launched a wave of ransomware attacks targeting the Russian economy, particularly focusing on energy and mining companies, financial services, and large retail chains, as disclosed on 19 October 2024. The group utilized a range of tools in addition to the ransomware of the LockBit 3.0 and Babuk strand, including Mimikatz, XenAllPasswordPro, PingCastle, AnyDesk, and various proxy services, to gain persistent access to compromised systems. The typical attack began with the use of stolen VPN access credentials, often obtained from subcontractors, allowing the attackers to remain undetected in the system for weeks, exfiltrating sensitive data using tools like MiniDump. The attacks culminated in the widespread encryption of all accessible data, including deleted files and virtual machines on ESXi servers. The tactics employed by Crypt Ghouls bear a strong resemblance to those of other ransomware groups, such as MorLock, BlackJack, and Twelve, leading experts to suspect a possible exchange of knowledge and resources among these groups.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Hijacked Virtual Machines at a Disaster Recovery Centre in Bengaluru, India, Forcing the Shutdown of Government Websites on 18 October 2024
column3: Unknown threat actors breached networks of the disaster recovery (DR) centre in Bengaluru, managed by a private company. Pivoting from this entry point to the Uttarakhand IT Development Agency's (ITDA) data centre in Dehradun, the threat actors deployed ransomware, forcing the shutdown of 192 government websites in the state, as reported by Nitika Khandelwal, director of ITDA, on 18 October 2024. A show-cause notice has been issued to the company managing the DR centre, following chief minister Pushkar Singh Dhami's directives. As of 18 October 2024, 160 of the affected websites have been restored, while 32 remained offline due to outdated systems and expired software licences.

column3: The ransomware gang Medusa Ransomware Group compromised the Providence Public School District in the US state of Rhode Island during September 2024. The group leaked information about students, including sexual misconduct allegations, special education records, and vaccine histories, after the school district did not pay a $1 million ransom demand. The over 337,000 leaked files, which include details about district investigations into serious complaints, as well as the names and home addresses of students, teachers, and parents, were uploaded to Telegram. The breach was first detected on 11 September 2024.

column2: Ransomware gang Hunters International breached Omni Family Health in the United States, stealing sensitive information in August 2024
column3: The Hunters International ransomware gang compromised Omni Family Health, a nonprofit organization providing healthcare services to communities in California. The group obtained personal information of nearly 470,000 current and former patients and employees in the breach. Omni Family Health discovered the intrusion on 7 August 2024, following claims that information was taken from its systems and leaked on the dark web. The data breach may have exposed varying personal information, including names, addresses, Social Security numbers, dates of birth, health insurance details, and medical information. The Hunters International ransomware gang claimed responsibility for the attack, stating they stole 2.7 terabytes of data, which was released on 23 August. The incident was officially disclosed by Omni Family Health on 19 October 2024.

column2: The North Korean Hacker Group Citrine Sleet Stole $51.5 Million from Radiant Capital Loan Protocol Users by Exploiting Smart Contract Vulnerabilities on 16 October 2024
column3: On 16 October 2024, the North Korean hacker group Citrine Sleet exploited a series of vulnerabilities in the smart contracts of the Radiant Capital loan protocol, manipulating smart contracts on two blockchains, BNB Smart Chain and Arbitrum, and gaining access to three private keys of signatories to a multi-signing wallet, which controlled the functions of the protocol, resulting in the theft of about $51.5 million from protocol users. The attack was initially reported by De.Fi, another security company, who warned users of the attack through a post on X, clarifying that changes to smart contracts had taken place fraudulently. In response to the attack, security company Ancilia inadvertently posted a link to a wallet drainer, while trying to assist victims, which was promptly removed after the community noticed the error. The incident was widely reported and commented on by security experts, who highlighted the importance of clear and transparent information by security companies, and the need for a strict verification protocol and responsible communications. Radiant Capital claimed to be aware of the problem and took steps to cooperate with several security experts, including Mandiant, SEAL911, Hyperactive, ZeroShadow and Chainalysis, to investigate and resolve the crisis. On 6th December, the Radiant Capital released a summary of the technical report by Mandiant on Medium including an attribution of the attack to the North Korean hacker group Citrine Sleet.

column2: Russia-linked threat actor RomCom targeted Ukrainian government agencies and Polish entities since late 2023
column3: Cisco Talos researchers observed Russia-linked threat actor RomCom (aka UAT-5647, Storm-0978, Tropical Scorpius, UAC-0180, UNC2596) targeting Ukrainian government agencies and Polish entities in a new wave of attacks since at least late 2023. In the recent attacks, RomCom deployed an updated variant of the RomCom RAT dubbed ‘SingleCamper. The infection chain consists of a spear-phishing message delivering a downloader consisting of either of two variants: “RustyClaw” – a RUST-based downloader, and a C++ based variant we track as “MeltingClaw”. The downloaders make way for and establish persistence for two distinct backdoors we call “DustyHammock” and “ShadyHammock,” respectively. The group uses multiple tools and malware languages (GoLang, C++, RUST, LUA) to establish long-term access for espionage, possibly followed by ransomware deployment for disruption and profit.

column2: North Korean State-Sponsored Hacking Group APT37 Stole Advertising Content From a South Korean Ad Agency to Target End Users in 2024
column3:
The North Korean state-sponsored hacking group APT37 (aka "TA-Red Ant," "Red Eyes," "ScarCruft," and "Group123") launched a cyber operation against a South Korean advertising agency and ultimately against end users, exploiting a previously unknown vulnerability in Microsoft Internet Explorer (IE), support for which had ended in June 2022.
The campaign took advantage of a flaw in the "Toast" advertising program. APT37 hacked into the servers of the aforementioned unnamed South Korean advertising agency, stealing ad programs and ad content and then injecting malicious code into the content, which would enable remote code execution when the ad is run on a victim's computer. These ads, which appear as pop-up notifications from the bottom right of a PC screen, need not be clicked on for the malicious code to be downloaded to a visitor's PC, according to AhnLab and the South Korean National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC)'s 16 October 2024 report. Following the discovery of the vulnerability in August 2024, AhnLab and the NCSC informed Microsoft, which distributed security patches on the vulnerability (CVE-2024-38178) in the same month.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors exploited Ivanti Cloud Services Appliance (CSA) in the United States from 13 September 2024 to 10 October 2024
column3: An alleged nation-state actor exploited three zero-day vulnerabilities in Ivanti Cloud Service Appliance (CSA) in recent attacks, specifically CVE-2024-9380, CVE-2024-8190, and CVE-2024-8963, to gain unauthenticated access to the CSA, enumerate users, and attempt to access their credentials. The actor then used obtained credentials to exploit a command injection flaw and deploy a web shell. The vulnerabilities were exploited in the wild against a limited number of customers, with the first exploitation reported on 13 September 2024. Ivanti warned of the vulnerabilities and recommended customers to upgrade to CSA 5.0.2 to fix the issues. The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) added the vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, ordering federal agencies to fix the vulnerabilities by 10 October 2024.

column2: Iranian State-Sponsored Hacking Group OilRig Exploited Windows Kernel Privilege Escalation Flaw in Cyber Espionage Campaign Targeting the United Arab Emirates in 2024
column3: The Iranian threat actor known as OilRig (also referred to as APT34, Crambus, Cobalt Gypsy, GreenBug, Hazel Sandstorm, and Helix Kitten) exploited a now-patched privilege escalation flaw impacting the Windows Kernel (CVE-2024-30088) as part of a cyber espionage campaign targeting the U.A.E. and the broader Gulf region in 2024. The group utilized sophisticated tactics that included deploying a backdoor that leverages Microsoft Exchange servers for credentials theft, and exploiting vulnerabilities for privilege escalation. Trend Micro researchers Mohamed Fahmy, Bahaa Yamany, Ahmed Kamal, and Nick Dai disclosed the attack in an analysis published on 13 October 2024. The attackers gained initial access to target networks by infiltrating a vulnerable web server to drop a web shell, followed by dropping the ngrok remote management tool to maintain persistence and move to other endpoints in the network. The attackers then used the privilege escalation vulnerability to deliver the backdoor, codenamed STEALHOOK, responsible for transmitting harvested data via the Exchange server to an email address controlled by the attacker. The attackers also used the elevated privileges to drop the password filter policy DLL (psgfilter.dll) in order to extract sensitive credentials from domain users via domain controllers or local accounts on local machines.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Compromised Iranian Government Sites and Nuclear Facilities in Iran on 12 October 2024
column3: Unknown threat actors, suspected to be Israel, compromised Iran's government institutions, nuclear industry, fuel distribution and transport networks, municipalities and ports, in a massive cyber attack on 12 October 2024, amid escalating Middle East tensions. The attack, which was described as 'unprecedented and enormous' by Abolhassan Firouzabadi, former secretary of the Supreme Council of Iran for cyberspace, resulted in the theft of a large amount of information. The attack was reportedly a surgical strike, involving sophisticated malware engineered to evade detection, and was likely designed to cripple multiple layers of Iran's infrastructure. The attack was attributed to Israel due to the country's history of cyber offensives against Iran and its vested interest in destabilising Iran's infrastructure. The incident was reported by various sources, including Iranian officials and cybersecurity experts, and was described as a 'precision strike' on Iranian systems. The attack has raised concerns about the escalating cyber warfare between Israel and Iran, and the potential for similar attacks on other countries.

The Internet Archive has been working to restore services and has advised users to change their passwords.

column2: Hacker Group GoldenJackal Gained Access Using the GoldenHowl Backdoor and Stole Unspecified Data Using the GoldenRobo Exfiltrator on a Network of a South Asian Embassy in Belarus in August 2019

column2: Chinese Hacking Group CeranaKeeper Stole Large Amounts of Data from the Government of Thailand by Using Cloud and File-Sharing Services for Infiltration Beginning in 2023
column3: The Chinese-related APT group, CeranaKeeper, targeted state institutions in Thailand in 2023, leaking a large amount of data. The group constantly updates its backdoors to avoid detection and diversify its methods to facilitate the mass data leak. CeranaKeeper used cloud and file sharing services, such as Dropbox, PixelDrain, GitHub, and OneDrive, for infiltration. The group also used services like Pastebin, Dropbox, OneDrive, and Github to conduct commands and release sensitive documents on previously compromised computers. The attacks were attributed to CeranaKeeper, a threat actor believed to be active since at least 2022, primarily targeting state institutions in Thailand, Myanmar, the Philippines, Japan, and Taiwan. The group's tactics, techniques, and procedures, code, and infrastructure were studied by ESET, which presented its findings on CeranaKeeper and the security deficit in Thailand at the 2024 Virus Bulletin conference. CeranaKeeper is probably using a public tool set called 'instruction Stager' (or 'TONESHELL') and has been observed using a sideloading technique to leak files from a network. The group also used a tool to empty its identity information and a legal Avast driver to disable security products in the machine. ESET researchers discovered several targeted campaigns that began in 2023 against state institutions in Thailand, where large amounts of data were leaked. CeranaKeeper's true identity and motivations are unknown, but its activities have been linked to China.

column3:
The Russian state-sponsored hacker group "NoName057(16)" launched a DDoS attack on several government websites and on several infrastructure targets in Belgium between 7 and 8 October 2024, justifying its attack due to Belgium's planned donation of three Caesar artillery units; while NoName declared the attack on its Telegram channel, it was confirmed by the Belgian Cybersecurity Centre (CBC) and the affected institutions.

column2: Chinese state-linked hacker group Salt Typhoon (aka FamousSparroww and GhostEmperor) breached wiretapping systems of US internet providers beginning August 2024
column3:
The threat actor Salt Typhoon, aka FamousSparroww and GhostEmporor, and linked to the Chinese government, allegedly infiltrated networks of various US broadband communications companies, including AT&T, Verizon, and Lumen Technologies, to access information about the system used by the US federal government to access telecommunication networks for court-authorized wiretapping requests.
The incident was disclosed in an exclusive reporting by the Wall Street Journal and is believed to have been conducted for reconaissance purposes. However, the full extent of the operation is still unclear and under investigation by the US government. The incident shares similarities to one conducted by the same threat actor that became publicly known two weeks earlier.
On 25 October 2024, the Wall Street Journal reported that "people familiar with the matter" said that the hackers targeted the phones of former President Donald Trump, his running mate, JD Vance, and persons who are affiliated with the campaign by democratic candidate Kamala Harris. However, at the time of reporting, the actual success of the infiltration attempts could not be verified.
On November 13, the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) and the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) published the same press release. They reported on PRC-affiliated actors compromising U.S. telecommunications operators.
On November 15, the Wall Street Journal reported that T-Mobile had also been affected by the cyber incident.
On November 27, 2024, T-Mobile issued a statement denying that the cyberattack had affected it. T-Mobile wrote that a T-Mobile wireline provider had been compromised, from where the hackers also attempted to infiltrate T-Mobile's network.
On 3 December 2024, US administration officials reported that they were still in the process of removing the hackers from the networks.
On 3 December 2024, senior officials from the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) and FBI briefed reporters on the ongoing investigation, describing it as a broad and significant campaign. The hackers accessed call metadata, targeted communications content from political and government figures, and data from law enforcement wiretap portals, though the latter was not the primary focus of the campaign. The investigation began earlier this year and has involved collaboration with numerous telecom providers.
On 4 December 2024, the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), NSA, FBI, and their counterparts in Australia, Canada, and New Zealand issued a joint warning about this cyber espionage campaign by the People's Republic of China (PRC)-affiliated threat actors targeting global telecommunications networks. The agencies emphasize that these compromises exploit known vulnerabilities, and they urge telecommunications providers, critical infrastructure organizations, and enterprises with on-premises equipment to adopt best practices outlined in their guide to enhance visibility, secure network devices, and mitigate further intrusions through timely patching and robust security measures.
On the same day, on 4 December 2024, the White House provided an update on China's "Salt Typhoon" hacking campaign, confirming that at least eight U.S. telecommunications companies were breached over the past one to two years, with similar attacks reported in "a couple of dozen" countries. The hackers accessed a significant amount of Americans' metadata, focusing on the communications of government officials and politicians, though no classified communications are believed to have been compromised.
On 27 December 2024, Deputy National Security Advisor for Cyber and Emerging Technologies Anne Neuberger provided an update on the cyber incident at a press briefing. She announced that the assistance provided by the government agencies after the cyber incident was first discovered in the summer of 2023 led to the identification of a ninth case involving an unnamed telecommunications company. Neuberger also reported that in one of the nine cases, the hackers gained access to an administrator account and thereby gained access to over 100,000 routers.
On 29 December 2024, the victim, Verizon Communications, reported in a statement emailed to TechCrunch that the cyber incident targeted a small number of high-profile customers in the government. On the following day, 30 December 2024, the victim AT&T also announced via TechCrunch that in their case, too, a small number of individuals of interest to foreign intelligence services were targeted and, in relatively few cases, information was compromised. Both telecommunications companies reported at the time of their statements that the cyber incident had been contained. On 31 December 2024, the telecommunications company Lumen Technologies reported that they had removed the hackers from their networks and that there was no evidence that access to data had been gained.
On 4 January 2024, the Wall Street Journal reported that Charter Communications, Consolidated Communications, and Windstream, in addition to the already known telecommunications companies, had also fallen victim to this cyber incident. The Wall Street Journal also wrote that the hackers exploited unfixed network devices from Fortinet and compromised large network routers.
Reuters reported on January 22, 2025, that the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) terminated all members of its advisory committees, according to a letter signed by Acting Secretary Benjamine C. Huffman and seen by Reuters. Among the affected committees is the Cyber Safety Review Board, which examines this cyberespionage operation by Salt Typhoon. Democratic Senator Ron Wyden criticized the decision, calling it a "gift to Chinese spies" and accusing DHS of undermining a critical investigation into a major security breach.

column2: Suspected Chinese state-sponsored hacking group APT10 targeted Japanese critical infrastructure sectors in Japan from December 2019 to 2022
column3: The Chinese state-sponsored hacking group APT10, also known as 'Earth Kasha' and 'MirrorFace', compromised Japanese companies in the manufacturing sector in December 2019, as part of a campaign dubbed 'Cuckoo Spear'. The attackers used advanced malware, including 'NOOPLDR' and 'NOOPDOOR', to gain persistent access to the victims' networks for up to three years. The malware was loaded using various techniques, including DLL side-loading and C# code execution with phishing as an initial access vector. The campaign was attributed to APT10 based on similarity in techniques employed by the Threat Actor. The attackers' intention was likely espionage, as they targeted critical infrastructure sectors and academic institutions.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors gained access to I-MED Radiology patient portal in late September 2024
column3: Unknown threat actors gained online access to the platform of the Australian radiology system 'I-MED Radiology' and stole physical data carriers with over 100,000 patient data records from medical practices in September 2024, as reported by an unknown source to Australian outlet Crikey. The hacker gained access to I-MED Radiology using credentials from a previous data leak to accounts not protected by multi-factor authentification. Stolen files contain full names, dates of birth, gender and, in some cases, MRI scans. Among the victims, the Thorax Center Münnerstadt in Southern Germany reported that it had been affected and its operations were currently only possible to a limited extent. Moreover, German police confirmed that data carriers were stolen from two medical practices in Hamburg with investigations are reportedly underway.

column2: North Korean state-sponsored hacking group Stonefly launched an attack on US companies in August 2024
column3:
The North Korean state-sponsored hacking group 'Stonefly' (also known as the North Korean Surveillance Patrol hack group, Andriel, APT45, Sailant Garima or Orix Slit) attacked at least three US
companies in August 2024, according to a report released by the cybersecurity company, Symantec. Stonefly used a custom malformation program called BlackDoor.Preft in this attack. The group has significantly improved its capabilities since 2019, focusing on high-value targets with sensitive information and/or know-how with a primarily financial motivation. The incident is part of Stonefly's continued monetary activities, despite arrest warrants and rewards offered by the US justice department against its members.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors Compromised IT Infrastructure of Uttarakhand Government Offices In India By Using Malware On 4 October 2024
column3: Unknown threat actors compromised the IT infrastructure of the Uttarakhand government offices in India on 4 October 2024, by using malware, which was detected on systems at the State Data Centre, leaving government offices unable to carry out work. The malware affected critical services like Secure Internet Service and the State Wide Area Network (SWAN), government websites, the Chief Minister’s helpline and land registration services. The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) and the National Informatics Centre (NIC) were informed about the attack. Experts at the Information Technology Development Agency (ITDA) are working to combat the malware. The State Wide Area Network (SWAN) services were restored in the first phase, but other services remained offline as of 4 October 2024.

column2: North Korean state-sponsored hacking group APT37 delivered a previously undocumented backdoor and remote access trojan called VeilShell to targets in Cambodia in 2024
column3: North Korean state-sponsored hacking group APT37 compromised targets in Cambodia and potentially other Southeast Asian countries in a campaign dubbed SHROUDED#SLEEP, using a previously undocumented backdoor and remote access trojan (RAT) called VeilShell, as reported by Securonix on 3 October 2024. The group delivered the malware via spear-phishing emails, allowing the attacker full access to the compromised machine, including data exfiltration, registry, and scheduled task creation or manipulation. The attack chain featured a lesser-known technique called AppDomainManager injection and used a PowerShell-based malware to contact a command-and-control (C2) server to await further instructions. Securonix researchers Den Iuzvyk and Tim Peck disclosed the ongoing operation in a technical report on 3 October 2024.

column2: 24-Year-Old Hacker Arrested for Compromising Italian Ministry of Justice and Major Corporations Between 2020-2024
column3: A 24-year-old hacker from Gela, currently residing in Rome, was arrested by the Postal Police on 1 October 2024 after a lengthy investigation into unauthorised access to the computer systems of the Ministry of Justice, Guardia di Finanza, Tim, and Telespazio. Between 2020 and 2024, the hacker acquired sensitive investigation files, including those related to organised crime, as disclosed by the National Anti-Mafia Directorate and the cybercrime pool of the Public Prosecutor's Office of Naples in a press conference on 2 October 2024. The arrest was facilitated by investigators who monitored the hacker's access to the servers, allowing them to seize a substantial amount of decrypted data. The hacker, who employed at least five fictitious identities, had the capability to control the information systems of justice, posing a serious threat to national security. The investigation involved multiple prosecutors and was coordinated by the National Anti-Mafia Directorate and the cybercrime unit of Naples.

column2: Unknown Threat Actor Compromised Public Wi-Fi at Major UK Railway Stations, Displaying Terror Messages on 25 September 2024
column3: On 25 September 2024, an unknown threat actor breached the public Wi-Fi networks at major railway stations in the UK, impacting 19 stations, including Manchester Piccadilly, Birmingham New Street, and 10 stations in London. Passengers attempting to connect were met with alarming messages about terror attacks in Europe, including a webpage titled "We love you, Europe." Network Rail, British Transport Police, and Telent, the third-party firm providing Wi-Fi, reported the incident, which was logged with authorities at approximately 5:03 p.m. Following the breach, the Wi-Fi services were taken offline nationwide, with restoration expected by the weekend. Network Rail assured that no personal data appeared to have been compromised.

column2: Likely state-backed threat actors hacked a police account and stole data on all Dutch police officers in a data breach in 2024
column3: Unknown threat actors compromised a police account and stole data on all Dutch police officers, including names, email addresses, and positions of the entire organisation, capturing also the names, official contact details, e-mail addresses, positions, and ranks of all Dutch police officers, including those working undercover. The perpetrators hacked a police account, which gave access to the main file of the entire police organisation, as disclosed by the Minister of Justice and Security, David van Weel, to the parliament in The Hague on 27 September 2024, with the exact number of people affected and the extent of the data leak still unknown. The security breach has been reported to the Data Protection Authority. The incident has raised concerns among police officers, with the main Dutch police union expressing worry about its members' safety and the potential consequences of the data leak. The criminal investigation is ongoing, with the police and national security partners trying to identify the attackers and prevent further harm.

column3: Lancaster Royal Grammar School in the United Kingdom was the target of a serious ransomware cyberattack on July 16, 2024, but the school did not interact with any demands. The attack is part of a series of similar ransomware attacks that affected other schools in the Fylde area, including the Fylde Coast Academy Trust, which was hit by a ransomware attack in September 2024, affecting 10 schools and forcing them to revert to manual processes.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors breached the systems and devices of the Kuwait Ministry of Health in Kuwait from 22 to 25 September 2024
column3: Unknown Threat Actors attempted to breach the confidentiality, integrity or availability of the Kuwait Ministry of Health's systems and devices in Kuwait from 22 to 25 September 2024, impacting several health facilities and administrative systems. The Ministry of Health took immediate action to ensure the continuity of essential health services and implemented measures to work with pre-prepared printed documents while safeguarding data integrity. The ministry coordinated with relevant government security agencies, which assisted in containing the problem and preventing its escalation. Infected systems were promptly disconnected from the network, while protection measures were enhanced through software updates and strict access control procedures. The attack did not compromise or encrypt the main databases. The Ministry of Health successfully restored its main systems and hospital operations from the first day of the incident and managed to decode the attack and recover its systems using secure backups. The Ministry of Health is collaborating with government security agencies to restore the remaining systems, which are expected to be fully operational soon.

column2: Unknown Threat Actors compromised the water treatment facility of Arkansas City, Kansas, USA, via ransomware attack on Sunday, 22 September 2024, forcing the facility to switch to manual operations
column3: Unknown Threat Actors launched a ransomware attack on the Arkansas City water treatment facility in Kansas, USA, on Sunday, 22 September 2024, forcing the facility to switch to manual operations. The attackers, believed to be a ransomware group, successfully encrypted at least some computer systems and likely stole information from the water facility, with an apparent ransom note appearing on a water treatment facility computer demanding the employee contact the attackers via email. The city has notified relevant authorities, including Homeland Security and FBI agents, who are investigating the incident. Cybersecurity experts and government authorities are working to resolve the situation and return the facility to normal operations. Enhanced security measures are currently in place to protect the water supply, and no changes to water quality or service are expected for residents. The water supply remains completely safe, and there has been no disruption to service, according to city officials.

column2: Iranian state-sponsored Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) intercepted data and sent 15,000 text messages to Swedish telecommunications network in Sweden on August 1, 2023
column3: Iran's security service, via the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), carried out a cyberattack against targets in Sweden on August 1, 2023, compromising a Swedish company that runs a major SMS service in Sweden and sending 15,000 text messages urging Swedes to take revenge against individuals who had burned the Quran, in an attempt to create divisions in Swedish society and portray Sweden as an Islamophobic country. The attack was attributed to Iran by the Swedish Public Prosecutor's Office and the Swedish Security Service, who identified the Iranian hackers responsible for the breach. However, the investigation was closed due to the lack of conditions for prosecution abroad or extradition to Sweden. The incident occurred in the wake of several Quran burnings in public places in Sweden, which sparked widespread anger in the Muslim world and generated diplomatic protests, riots, and threats of economic boycott. The Swedish government had condemned the actions, but the country's courts allowed the protests to proceed under Sweden's freedom of expression laws.

column2: Unknown threat actors attacked MoneyGram in September 2024 and stole data
column3:
Unknown threat actors attacked MoneyGram in September 2024 affecting several systems. After several customers complaints about the lack of services since Friday 20 September, the company itself confirmed a cybersecurity incident. Due to the attack they shut their systems down, impacting network connectivity. Several services like the MoneyGram App or sending and receiving payments are currently unavailable.
MoneyGram released a statement on October 8, 2024, saying that an unknown party gained unauthorized access and stole personal information from September 20 to 22, 2024. This included names, contact information (such as phone numbers, email and postal addresses), dates of birth, a limited number of Social Security numbers, copies of government-issued identification documents (such as driver’s licenses), other identification documents (such as utility bills), bank account numbers, MoneyGram Plus Rewards numbers, transaction information (such as dates and amounts of transactions) and, for a limited number of consumers, criminal investigation information (such as fraud).
A source familiar with the attack shared further information, telling BleepingComputer that MoneyGram was initially breached through a social engineering attack on the company's internal help desk. This attack allowed the threat actors to access MoneyGram's network using an employee's credentials and target employee information in the company's Windows Active Directory Services.

column2: The Russian state-sponsored hacking group NoName057(16) disrupted several official websites of Austrian political parties and institutions beginning on 23 September 2024
column3:
The Russian state-sponsored hacking group NoName057(16) disrupted several official websites of Austrian political parties and institutions beginning on 23 September 2024, the chief executive officer of the detective agency Foreus, Stefan Embacher, attributed in an article by the television station OE24 on 24 September.
On Monday, 23 September 2024, the websites of the Austrian People's Party (ÖVP) and the Social Democratic Party of Austria (SPÖ) were disrupted. On 24 September 2024, the website of the Communist Party of Austria (KPÖ) was also disrupted. On 25 September 2024, the Ministry of Defense, the Austrian Court of Auditors, and other unnamed websites were disrupted.
Embacher said that NoName057(16) is a hacker group with "close ties to the Kremlin" and that they are "financed by Putin's regime." In an immediate exchange with the hacker group, they confirmed they had targeted Austrian political parties in the upcoming elections on Sunday, 29 September 2024.

column2: China-Linked Earth Baxia Targeted APAC Using Spear-Phishing and GeoServer Exploit Starting July 2024
column3: Earth Baxia, a threat actor linked to China, has initiated a sophisticated cyber campaign targeting entities across the Asia-Pacific (APAC) region. In July 2024, the group leveraged spear-phishing techniques to deliver malicious payloads and subsequently exploited the critical Remote Code Execution (RCE) vulnerability CVE-2024-36401 in GeoServer, an open-source Java-based server that manages and disseminates geospatial data. This exploit allowed for deep infiltration into targeted networks, enabling data exfiltration and persistent access while deploying customised Cobalt Strike payloads. Trend Micro reported the use of a new backdoor called EAGLEDOOR, which enhances the threat actor's information gathering and payload delivery capabilities.

column2: Unknown Hackers Deploy Ransomware Against UK-Based Fylde Coast Academy Trust Since September 2024
column3:
In September 2024, unknown threat actors launched a ransomware attack targeting the Fylde Coast Academy Trust (FCAT) in Blackpool, disrupting the operations of all 10 schools under its management. The attack compromised the Trust's IT infrastructure, rendering critical systems inaccessible, including those used for teaching, maintaining records, and communication.
All 10 institutions within the FCAT umbrella have been impacted. Among them are four key academies in Blackpool: Armfield Academy, Aspire Academy, Montgomery Academy, and Unity Academy. Additionally, Mereside, Gateway, Westcliff, and Westminster primary schools, along with Garstang Community Academy and Hambleton Primary Academy, are also managing the repercussions.
As a result, the schools were forced to revert to non-digital processes, significantly impacting daily operations. The attackers demanded a substantial ransom, threatening to permanently block access to the compromised systems if their demands were not met.

column2: Unknown Perpetrators Rendered the Website of the Austrian City of Graz Inaccessible with DDoS Attack since 20 September 2024
column3:
On 20 September 2024, the city of Graz experienced a significant cyber disruption when its official website was targeted by a DDoS attack. The assault, which began early in the morning, flooded the site with an overwhelming number of server requests, causing it to crash and become inaccessible to users. Despite the ongoing nature of the attack, city officials assured the public that the security of their data and the integrity of the city’s systems were not compromised.
Throughout the day, IT teams worked intensively to counter the attack and restore normal website operations. However, by the afternoon, the website continued to experience intermittent outages, and the timeline for full recovery remained unclear. Sarah Tatschl, a spokesperson from the city's Public Relations office, emphasized that although the service disruption was inconvenient, it did not pose any risk to the data security of Graz's citizens.

column2: The North Korean state-sponsored hacking group UNC2970 gained access to a multinational energy company in June 2024
column3:
The North Korean state-sponsored hacking group UNC2970 gained access to a multinational energy company in June 2024, Google reported in a technical report on 17 September 2024.
The hacking group posed as a recruiter for well-known companies and lured an unspecified victim via WhatsApp with a job offer. The job offer was in an encrypted PDF file that the victim could only open with the help of a trojanised version of the SumatraPDF software, which downloaded the MISTPEN backdoor via the BURNBOOK uploader. The specific victim downloaded such a ZIP archive, and the connection to the backdoor was then made via a SharePoint server at an unnamed university.

column2: The Australian Federal Police (AFP), alone and in cooperation with other law enforcement agencies in Operation Task Force (OTF) NEXT, gained access to devices using the messaging service Ghost beginning in March 2022
column3:
The Australian Federal Police (AFP), alone and in cooperation with other law enforcement agencies in Operation Task Force (OTF) NEXT, gained access to devices using the messaging service Ghost from March 2022 to 18 September 2024, as Europol and the AFP reported on 18 September 2024.
As part of a separate law enforcement operation, Operation Kraken, under the OTF NEXT, the Australian Federal Police modified the updates from the Ghost messaging service operator to gain access to devices in Australia that used this messaging service. The Ghost messaging service was a communication platform used for illegal activities. However, OTF NEXT also spoke of technical interventions that allowed participating law enforcement agencies to access messages shared via Ghost.
In addition to many law enforcement actions in many countries between 17 and 18 September 2024, including arrests and seizures, AFP charged the operator of the messaging service, 32-year-old Jay Je Yoon Jung of New South Wales, with five counts.

column2: FBI Disrupts Global Botnet Operated by Chinese State-Sponsored Hackers That Affected Over 200,000 Devices Worldwide
column3:
On September 18, 2024, the U.S. Department of Justice announced the successful disruption of a botnet that had infected more than 200,000 consumer devices across the United States and globally. The botnet was operated by state-sponsored hackers from the People’s Republic of China (PRC), specifically working for Integrity Technology Group, a Beijing-based company known in the cybersecurity community as “Flax Typhoon.” The botnet compromised a wide range of consumer devices, including small-office/home-office (SOHO) routers, IP cameras, DVRs, and network-attached storage (NAS) devices. These infected devices were linked together into a network controlled by Integrity Technology Group, which used them to conduct covert cyber activities under the guise of normal internet traffic. In a court-authorized operation, the FBI took control of the hackers’ infrastructure and issued disabling commands to the malware across the infected devices.
During the operation, the hackers attempted to disrupt the FBI’s efforts with a DDoS attack, but the FBI successfully neutralized this threat, ensuring the botnet's dismantlement.

column2: Pro-Russian hacktivist group NoName057(16) targeted Taiwanese Stock Exchange alongside 45 other entities in DDoS campaign starting on 11 September 2024
column3: During 11-15 September 2024, the pro-Russian hacker group ‘NoName057(16)’ carried out a series of DDoS attacks on 45 Taiwanese entities. The most prominent of these was carried out against the Taiwan Stock Exchange (TWSE), which left its website briefly unavailable after trading hours. The disruptions, which also targeted other government and financial websites, came in response to recent remarks by Taiwanese President William Lai. Lai had suggested that China should reclaim land from Russia's Far East, which the hacktivist group cited as a reason for its campaign. NoName057(16) reported support for its activities from the hacker collective RipperSec, that previously engaged in pro-Palestinian operations. While the TWSE website experienced delays, the exchange clarified that the incident did not affect trading systems. The hackers aimed to disrupt the websites of a range of Taiwanese organizations, with similar DDoS attempts reported for airports and local tax bureaus. Taiwan's Minister of Digital Affairs Yennun Huang linked NoName057(16) and RipperSec to the campaign in a press briefing on 14 September.

column2: An unknown threat actor gained unauthorized access to an Azure Sharepoint instance of Fortinet on September 2024
column3: Cybersecurity giant Fortinet has confirmed it suffered a data breach after a threat actor with the moniker ‘Fortibitch’ claimed to steal 440GB of files from the company's Microsoft Sharepoint server. column2: An Iranian-nexus hacking group attacks Iraqi entities over several months in 2024
column3: An Iranian-nexus hacking group attacks Iraqi entities over several months in 2024. According to Check Point Research, this campaign targets different Iraqi entities, including government networks. This campaign features a custom toolset and infrastructure for specific targets and uses a combination of techniques commonly associated with Iranian threat actors operating in the region. This toolset employs command and control (C2) mechanisms, including a custom DNS tunnelling protocol and a tailor-made email based C2 channel. The C2 channel uses compromised email accounts within the targeted organization, indicating that the threat actor successfully infiltrated the victim’s networks. The Veaty and Spearal Tactics, Techniques and Procedures (TTPs) are very similar to two other malware families, Karkoff and Saitama, which are attributed to the same APT34 actor, indicating that this campaign is implemented by a similar nexus threat actor.

column2: Kremlin-Linked COLDRIVER aka Star Blizzard Launched Spear-Phishing Campaign Targeting Eastern European Civil Society and US-Based Free Russia Foundation Between April and September 2024
column3:
Between April and June 2024, the Kremlin-linked cyberespionage group COLDRIVER aka Star Blizzard conducted a spear-phishing campaign targeting civil society members, NGOs, and the US-based Free Russia Foundation, a pro-democracy think tank. The group focused on organizations operating in Eastern Europe, sending phishing emails containing malicious PDF links designed to compromise victims' systems.
COLDRIVER impersonated trusted contacts to lure targets into downloading the malicious PDFs. Once accessed, victims' devices were scanned, and data was transmitted to command-and-control servers hosted on virtual private servers from Hostinger International Limited. The group used Proton Mail accounts to maintain anonymity throughout the operation.
In addition to NGOs and media outlets in Russia and Belarus, the campaign notably targeted the Free Russia Foundation in the beginning of September 2024, leading to the theft of over 2,500 email chains and 13GB of sensitive documents. These included grant reports, internal correspondence, and personal information, which were subsequently leaked on a Telegram channel. The leak is believed to be linked to COLDRIVER’s continued efforts to destabilize pro-democracy entities.

column2: Chinese-speaking threat cluster TIDRONE targets Taiwanese Military and Satellite Industries with malware toolsets CXCLNT and CLNTEND since April 2024
column3:
According to a report by Trend Micro, the Chinese-speaking threat cluster TIDRONE targets Taiwanese Military and Satellite Industries with malware toolsets CXCLNT and CLNTEND since April 2024. Before that, TIDRONE activities also focused on unspecified entities in Korea and Canada, but also payment services in Taiwan. Trend Micro's research indicates the attackers have certain interest in military-related industry chains, particularly in the manufacturers of drones, coming from Taiwan. The observed TTPs show a substantiate evolution of the group's malware arsenal and that they optimized their attack chain. According to the report, the group likely moved to the lateral movement stage at the time of reporting, while having used other techniques some time ago for initial infiltration. Furthermore, the group uses two backdoors throughout its operations, namely CXCLNT and CLNTEND.
Trend Micro states that the "consistency in file compilation times and the threat actor’s operation time with other Chinese espionage-related activities supports the assessment that this campaign is likely being carried out by an as-yet unidentified Chinese-speaking threat group". Given the sensitivity of data hold by the targeted drone manufacturers, espionage seems as the most reasonable operation motive.

column3: Transport for London's (TfL) computer systems have been targeted in an ongoing cyber attack in September 2024. Until now, there is no evidence customer data had been compromised and there was currently no impact on TfL services. However, the Dial-a-Ride assisted transit service for disabled people was temporarily suspended. One source close to the matter told the Register: "The TfL hack was their Cisco VPN getting popped." column2: North Korean Hacker Group Citrine Sleet Exploits Chromium Zero-Day Vulnerability on 19 August 2024
column3:
On 19 August 2024, the North Korean hacker group Citrine Sleet exploited the Chromium zero-day vulnerability (CVE-2024-7971) to conduct remote code execution (RCE) attacks on 19 August 2024. Targets were initially directed to the Citrine Sleet-controlled exploit domain voyagorclub[.]space. Upon connection, the zero-day RCE exploit for CVE-2024-7971 was deployed. Once code execution was achieved in the sandboxed Chromium renderer process, shellcode containing a Windows sandbox escape exploit and the FudModule rootkit was downloaded and executed in memory. The sandbox escape leveraged CVE-2024-38106, allowing the main FudModule rootkit to run in memory. This rootkit uses direct kernel object manipulation (DKOM) techniques to bypass kernel security mechanisms, operating entirely from user mode while performing kernel tampering via a kernel read/write primitive.
CVE-2024-7971 is a type confusion vulnerability in the V8 JavaScript and WebAssembly engine, affecting versions of Chromium prior to 128.0.6613.84. Google released a patch for this vulnerability on 21 August 2024, and users are strongly advised to update to the latest version of Chromium.
This marks the third exploited V8 type confusion vulnerability patched in V8 this year, following CVE-2024-4947 and CVE-2024-5274. In response to this nation-state actor activity, Microsoft has directly notified targeted or compromised customers, providing critical information to help secure their environments.

column2: Iran-based Pioneer Kitten Enabling Ransomware Attacks on US Organizations since September 2022
column3: The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), and the Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center (DC3) released a joint Cybersecurity Advisory (CSA) to warn network defenders that, as of August 2024, a group of Iran-based actors continues to exploit US and foreign organizations. This includes organizations across several sectors in the US (including in the education, finance, healthcare, and defense sectors as well as local government entities) and other countries (including in Israel, Azerbaijan, and the United Arab Emirates). The FBI assesses a significant percentage of these threat actors’ operations against US organizations are intended to obtain and develop network access to then collaborate with ransomware affiliate actors to deploy ransomware. The actors’ initial access is usually obtained via exploiting a public-facing networking device, such as Citrix Netscaler (CVE-2019-19781 and CVE-2023-3519), F5 BIG-IP (CVE-2022-1388), Pulse Secure/Ivanti VPNs (CVE-2024-21887), and, more recently, PanOS firewalls (CVE-2024-3400). After infiltrating victim networks, the actors collaborate with ransomware affiliates (including NoEscape, Ransomhouse, and ALPHV [aka BlackCat]) in exchange for a percentage of the ransom payments by providing affiliates with access to victim networks, locking victim networks, and strategizing to extort victims. The actors also conduct what is assessed to be a separate set of malicious activity—stealing sensitive data from victims, likely in support of the government of Iran.

column2: Iran-Linked APT33 Group Used New Tickler Malware to Backdoor US and UAE Organizations Between April and July 2024
column3:
Between April and July 2024, the Iran-linked cyberespionage group APT33, also known as Peach Sandstorm and Refined Kitten, conducted a series of infiltrations targeting critical organizations in the government, defense, satellite, and oil and gas sectors in the United States and the United Arab Emirates. The group deployed a new multi-stage backdoor malware named Tickler as part of these operations.
APT33 gained initial access to the targeted networks through password spray attacks, attempting to breach multiple accounts using a few commonly used passwords to evade detection. The attackers also used social engineering tactics, particularly focusing on the education sector to procure operational infrastructure, including setting up fraudulent Azure tenants using compromised accounts.
The Tickler malware, disguised as legitimate PDF files, was used to establish a foothold in the compromised systems. The malware collected network information and communicated with command-and-control (C2) servers hosted on Microsoft Azure infrastructure, which Microsoft later identified and disrupted.
In addition to maintaining persistence within the networks, the attackers utilized the SMB protocol for lateral movement, allowing them to move across the compromised systems. Microsoft has since shared indicators of compromise (IoCs). APT33’s activities are linked to intelligence collection efforts on behalf of the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC).
On 18 September 2024, Brad Smith, vice-chairman and president of Microsoft Corporation, reported to the United States Senate Select Committee on Intelligence that the US government sector affected was at least or exclusively a county government in a disputed swing state. The hacking group managed to access an account belonging to the county government, but it is unclear whether the incident was related to the elections.

column2: China-linked APT Volt Typhoon exploited a zero-day in Versa Director in June 2024
column3: China-linked APT Volt Typhoon exploited a zero-day vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2024-39717, in Versa Director, to deploy a custom webshell on breached networks, allowing the threat actors to intercept credentials in plain text. Versa Director is a centralized management and orchestration platform used primarily by Internet Service Providers (ISPs) and Managed Service Providers (MSPs) to manage and monitor Software-Defined Wide Area Networks (SD-WANs). Researchers at Lumen’s Black Lotus Labs discovered a zero-day vulnerability in Versa Director on June 17. Black Lotus Labs detected unusual traffic indicating the exploitation of several US Versa Director servers between June 12 and mid-July 2024. The initial access to these compromised systems was likely through port 4566, typically used for high-availability (HA) pairing between Versa nodes. Black Lotus analysis of global telemetry identified actor-controlled small-office/home-office (SOHO) devices exploiting this zero-day vulnerability at four US victims and one non-US victim in the Internet service provider (ISP), managed service provider (MSP) and information technology (IT) sectors. Black Lotus linked the activity to Volt Typhoon with moderate confidence.

column2: Rhysida ransomware group target Port of Seattle and Seattle-Tacoma International Airport (USA) with Ransomware on 24 August 2024
column3:
Rhysida ransomware group targeted the Port of Seattle, which also operates the Seattle-Tacoma (Sea-Tac) International Airport, with a cyberattack on 24 August 2024. The outages began early Saturday and took down several systems. While the full scale of the outage has not been made public, the Port of Seattle websites, email and phone systems were also affected. 
Security operations at Sea-Tac Airport were not impacted with 72,000 people flying out on Sunday, although the baggage sorting systems and some terminal screens at Sea-Tac Airport were disrupted. Travellers have been advised to allow extra time for their stay at the airport, use mobile applications for check-ins, and be prepared for longer-than-usual security lines and baggage claim wait times.
On 13 September, Port of Seattle confirmed that Rhysida ransomware group was behind the cyberattack. They also confirmed that Rhysida accessed and encrypted parts of their computer systems, disrupting key services like baggage handling, check-in kiosks, ticketing, Wi-Fi, and parking. Port of Seattle also stated that they refused to pay the ransom, for this reason the ransomware group may publish stolen data.

column2: China-nexus group Velvet Ant exploited zero-day vulnerability in Cisco switches for espionage campaign
column3: On 1 July 2024, the Israeli cybersecurity firm Sygnia disclosed its discovery of a China-nexus threat actor exploiting a zero-day vulnerability in Cisco network appliances. In subsequent reporting on 22 August, Sygnia detailed that the state-sponsored group Velvet Ant leveraged a command injection vulnerability in the command line interface of Cisco’s Nexus-series switches (CVE-2024-20399). The software flaw allowed the threat actors to gain direct access to the underlying Linux OS on which the operations of the switches are based. When used with valid administrator credentials to access the switch management console, the exploit enables threat actors to run arbitrary scripts on the compromised devices. Sygnia reported the vulnerability to Cisco in April 2024 after it had detected its abuse in the wild as part of an investigation of a multi-year espionage campaign conducted by Velvet Ant.

column2: Members of Ukraine's Main Intelligence Directorate targeted Russian communication and internet service providers on 24 August 2024
column3:
Members of Ukraine's Main Intelligence Directorate (GURMO) with assistance from Ukrainian hacktivists targeted Russian communication and Internet service providers on 24 August 2024.
According to Ukraine's state news agency Ukrinform, the operation was conducted to coincide with Ukraine's Independence Day on 24 August and was aimed at the Internet service provider Rostelecom as well as the mobile operators MTC and Beeline in addition to online services of Yandex. 
Additional targets included the Chaika-Service automobile factory, aircraft equipment producer LLC Kontur-NIIRS and the supplier of hardware and software supplier PJSC I.S. Brook INEUM, which support the Russian armed forces.
The operation allegedly also targeted the cloud and file storage of the Internet providers Mail.ru, VDSina, and Quidex as well as the resources of lighting equipment manufacturers, diesel stations, hosting providers, and ballistic protection manufacturers. Ukrinform cited GURMO sources that claimed the operation reached 33 target servers and 283 work stations at factories. According to these reports, 21 websites were shut down and 15 cloud and file storage repositories were deleted, erasing 70 terabytes of data.

column3:
Unknown attackers gained access to the systems of French Paris-Saclay University and deployed ransomware on 11 August 2024.
The incident impacted all internal servers of the university, causing outages to the institution's email services, the intranet, shared work spaces and certain business applications. The university communicated details of the intrusion on its website ten days after it was first detected, disclosing the involvement of ransomware. On October 9, the ransomware group RansomHouse claimed to have stolen 1 TB of data from the University of Paris-Saclay.

column2: Unknown attackers infected the online stores of hundreds of companies using the e-commerce platform Magento in August 2024
column3: Malwarebyte Labs reports unknown attackers infected the online stores of hundreds of companies using the e-commerce platform Magento with a digital skimmer in August 2024. column2: Unknown Hackers Breached McDonald’s Instagram Account in $700K Solana-Based Crypto Scam on 21 August 2024
column3:
On 21 August 2024, unknown hackers gained control of McDonald’s official Instagram account and the personal X account of the company's senior markeing director. The hackers used this access to promote a fraudulent Solana-based cryptocurrency scheme. The attackers posted links to a fake "Grimace" meme coin, tricking followers into transferring funds through the Solana blockchain, believing they were investing in a McDonald’s-branded cryptocurrency. The scam resulted in over $700,000 in stolen funds.
McDonald’s quickly regained control of its Instagram account and removed the fraudulent posts. The company issued an apology and is cooperating with law enforcement to investigate the breach.

column2: Akira Ransomware Group Steals and Encrypts Data from Switzerland's Hoerbiger Holding on 29 July 2024
column3:
On 29 July 2024, Swiss industrial company Hoerbiger Holding, known for its expertise in compression technology, drive technology, and safety systems, was targeted by the Akira ransomware group. The attackers infiltrated the company’s network, stealing and encrypting over 50 GB of sensitive data, including employee information, customer data, contracts, and financial records. The attack also disrupted production at several locations.
Hoerbiger confirmed that the ransomware attack compromised two out of 800 global servers, while core systems such as Microsoft 365, ERP, CRM, and HR platforms remained unaffected due to external hosting. In response, the company has restored its production facilities and is working to rebuild its IT infrastructure with strengthened security measures.

column2: Jesse Kipf, a 39-year-old US citizen from Kentucky, gained access to the Hawaii death registry and faked his death intending to escape his child support payments beginning in January 2023
column3:
Jesse Kipf, a 39-year-old US citizen from Kentucky, gained access to the Hawaii death registry and faked his death intending to escape his child support payments beginning in January 2023, the US Attorney's Office, Eastern District of Kentucky reported in its press release on 20 August 2024.
In the case of the Hawaii death registry, Kipf used the username and password of a doctor in another state to gain access to it. Kipf also gained unauthorised access to other unnamed death registries, private business networks, and government and corporate networks with the aim of selling access on the darknet.
The failure to make maintenance payments and the damage to the affected computer networks totalled $195,758.65.
The US District Court for the Eastern District of Kentucky sentenced Kipf to 81 months imprisonment for computer fraud and aggravated identity theft.

column2: Unknown Hacker Gained Control of US-Based Unicoin's Google G-Suite Accounts and Locked Out Employees on 9 August 2024
column3:
On 9 August 2024, a hacker gained unauthorized access to the Google G-Suite accounts of San Francisco-based cryptocurrency firm Unicoin, locking all employees out of key systems, including G-Mail and G-Drive, for nearly four days. The breach disrupted operations until 13 August 2024, when Unicoin regained control of its accounts.
While no evidence of stolen digital assets has been found, the breach led to discrepancies in the personal data of employees and contractors, particularly within the accounting department. Additionally, traces of hacked messages were identified in certain managers' email accounts. Unicoin continues to investigate the incident and its potential impact on operations, while also terminating a contractor who had forged their identity during the ongoing investigation.

column2: Chinese state-affiliated APT Earth Baku targeted public and private organizations in Italy, Germany, UAE, Qatar and Thailand with data theft since late 2022
column3:
Earth Baku, a hacking group associated with the Chinese-state-sponsored APT41, expanded its geographical reach by pursuing targets in Germany, Italy, United Arab Emirates, Qatar and Thailand since late 2022, according to a report by Trend Micro from 9 August 2024. Previously, the group had predominantly focused on the Indo-Pacific region. The group targeted the following sectors: government, media and communications, telecom, technology, healthcare and education.
Suspected but non-confirmed target geographies also included Georgia and Romania. Throughout those activities, Earth Baku updated its tactics, using public-facing applications like IIS servers as entry points for attacks. They then deploy sophisticated malware, including the loaders StealthVector and StealthReacher, and the modular backdoor SneakCross. In the post-exploitation stage, Earth Baku uses reverse tunneling tools to maintain control and deploys the MEGAcmd tool, likely for data exfiltration.
